401(k).def: Your employer's retirement account where you stash pre-tax money, watch it grow tax-free, and pay taxes only when you pull it out decades later. If your company matches what you put in, you're literally getting free money.
401(k).whyThisMatters: That employer match is an instant 50-100% return — crush that before you invest anywhere else.

52-Week High.def: The top price a stock hit over the last year.
52-Week High.whyThisMatters: When a stock smashes through its old high with heavy buying, it often keeps climbing — momentum is real.

52-Week Low.def: The rock-bottom price a stock sank to in the past 52 weeks.
52-Week Low.whyThisMatters: A stock at yearly lows might be a steal or a disaster — figure out if it's temporary panic or a dying business before you jump in.

Ask Price.def: The lowest price someone's willing to sell a stock for right now.
Ask Price.whyThisMatters: If you hit 'buy' without checking the ask on a sleepy stock, you might overpay by a lot — that gap between bid and ask can wreck you instantly.

Asset.def: Stuff you or a company owns that's worth money — cash, buildings, equipment, patents, whatever.
Asset.whyThisMatters: Good companies turn their assets into serious cash; bad ones pile up junk that earns nothing.

Asset Allocation.def: How you split your money between stocks, bonds, cash, and other stuff to balance risk and growth.
Asset Allocation.whyThisMatters: Your mix of asset types drives like 90%+ of your long-term returns — way more than picking individual stocks.

Balance Sheet.def: A snapshot showing what a company owns, what it owes, and what's left over for shareholders at one moment in time.
Balance Sheet.whyThisMatters: Companies with tons of cash and little debt can survive crashes and pounce when everyone else is broke.

Bear.whyThisMatters: Bears catch fraud and hype before everyone else — they keep markets honest even if they're annoying.

Bear Market.def: When stocks tank 20% or more from their peak and everyone's freaking out.
Bear Market.whyThisMatters: Bear markets suck but they're short (usually under a year) — keep buying and you'll grab stocks cheap before the rebound.

Beta.def: How wild a stock's price swings compared to the whole market. Above 1 means extra volatile, below 1 means calmer.
Beta.whyThisMatters: High-beta stocks rocket up in good times and crater in bad ones — know what you're signing up for before you size your bet.

Bid Price.def: The most a buyer's offering for a stock right now.
Bid Price.whyThisMatters: Check the bid before you sell anything illiquid or you might get way less cash than you expected.

Blue Chip Stock.def: Shares of huge, boring, rock-solid companies that've been around forever.
Blue Chip Stock.whyThisMatters: They won't 10x overnight, but they won't implode either — they hold up when markets crash and risky stuff dies.

Bond.def: You lend money to a company or government, they pay you interest for a set time, then give your cash back.
Bond.whyThisMatters: Bonds smooth out your portfolio's wild swings and pay you while you wait — just know that when rates rise, bond prices fall.

Book Value.def: What's left if a company sold everything and paid off all its debts — assets minus liabilities.
Book Value.whyThisMatters: Stocks trading way below book value might be bargains, but book value ignores brand power and other intangibles that actually print money.

Brokerage Account.def: The account you use to actually buy and sell stocks, bonds, ETFs, and other investments.
Brokerage Account.whyThisMatters: Taxable vs. IRA changes everything — stash high-turnover or dividend-heavy stuff in tax-advantaged accounts to keep way more of your gains.

Bull.whyThisMatters: When literally everyone's a bull, that's often a red flag — if everyone already bought, who's left to push prices higher?

Bull Market.def: A sustained rally where stocks climb 20%+ from the bottom and optimism's everywhere.
Bull Market.whyThisMatters: Bull markets last way longer and climb way higher than bear markets fall — staying in is how you actually make money long-term.

Call Option.def: A contract letting you buy a stock at a locked-in price before a deadline — you can use it or let it expire.
Call Option.whyThisMatters: Calls give you huge upside with capped risk (you can only lose what you paid), but most expire worthless because time kills them.

Capital.def: The money available to invest or run a business.
Capital.whyThisMatters: How well a company invests its capital determines whether it builds wealth or lights money on fire — check return on invested capital vs. cost of capital.

Capital Gain.def: The profit you pocket when you sell something for more than you paid.
Capital Gain.whyThisMatters: Hold over a year and you'll pay way less tax (long-term rates) than if you flip it quick — that 15-20% difference compounds like crazy.

Capital Loss.def: The hit you take when you sell for less than you paid.
Capital Loss.whyThisMatters: Losses offset gains at tax time — smart investors harvest losses to cut their tax bill and let winners compound.

Commodities.def: Raw stuff like oil, gold, wheat, copper that gets bought and sold on exchanges.
Commodities.whyThisMatters: Commodity prices drive inflation and corporate costs — when oil spikes, it squeezes consumers and kills margins across tons of industries at once.

Common Stock.def: Standard ownership in a company — you get voting rights and whatever's left after everyone else gets paid.
Common Stock.whyThisMatters: You're last in line if the company crashes, but you get unlimited upside if it crushes — that's why stocks beat bonds over time.

Compound Interest.def: Earning interest on your interest — your money snowballs exponentially instead of growing in a straight line.
Compound Interest.whyThisMatters: Start at 25 instead of 40 and your money compounds for nearly twice as long — time is your biggest advantage, use it.

Correction.def: A drop of 10%+ from a recent peak.
Correction.whyThisMatters: Corrections happen every year or two and they're totally normal — they create better buy-in prices if you keep your cool and stick to your plan.

Coupon Rate.def: The yearly interest rate printed on a bond, shown as a percent of face value.
Coupon Rate.whyThisMatters: The coupon tells you annual income, but yield-to-maturity (based on current price) is what actually determines your real return.

Cryptocurrency.def: Digital money secured by cryptography, usually running on decentralized blockchain networks.
Cryptocurrency.whyThisMatters: Crypto's wildly speculative with zero cash flows to anchor value — if you buy any, size it small because the swings are brutal.

Day Trading.def: Buying and selling stocks in the same day to profit from tiny price moves.
Day Trading.whyThisMatters: Almost everyone who day-trades loses money after fees and taxes — you're competing against pros with better tech and you'll probably get wrecked.

Debt-to-Equity Ratio.def: Total debt divided by shareholder equity — shows how levered up a company is.
Debt-to-Equity Ratio.whyThisMatters: Context matters — a ratio that's fine for a stable utility would be insanely risky for a cyclical manufacturer.

Default.def: When a borrower can't make required debt payments.
Default.whyThisMatters: Default torches access to credit, triggers other debt clauses, and in bad cases wipes out equity holders completely.

Delta.def: How much an option's price moves per $1 move in the stock — ranges from 0 to 1 for calls, -1 to 0 for puts.
Delta.whyThisMatters: A 0.5 delta means your option moves about 50 cents per dollar of stock movement — it tells you your real exposure.

Depreciation.def: Spreading the cost of equipment or buildings over their useful life — it cuts taxable income without using actual cash.
Depreciation.whyThisMatters: It's a non-cash charge, so add it back to get operating cash flow — heavy-equipment companies often show very different earnings vs. actual cash.

Derivative.def: A contract whose value comes from an underlying asset — options, futures, swaps, forwards.
Derivative.whyThisMatters: Leverage means you can make or lose way more than you put in — size carefully or get destroyed.

Diversification.def: Spreading your money across different investments so one blow-up doesn't wreck you.
Diversification.whyThisMatters: It's the only free lunch in investing — cuts risk without killing returns, so going concentrated better be backed by serious conviction.

Dividend.def: Cash a company pays you just for owning its stock, usually every quarter.
Dividend.whyThisMatters: Dividends have driven about 40% of all stock market gains historically — focus on dividend growth, not just high yield.

Dividend Yield.def: Annual dividend per share divided by the stock price, shown as a percent.
Dividend Yield.whyThisMatters: A crazy-high yield might mean bargain or dividend cut incoming — check if cash flow can actually support it before you chase yield.

Dollar-Cost Averaging.def: Investing the same dollar amount on a set schedule no matter what the price is — you buy more shares when it's cheap, fewer when it's expensive.
Dollar-Cost Averaging.whyThisMatters: It kills the impossible game of timing the market and makes it way easier to keep investing when everything's tanking.

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA).def: An index of 30 huge US companies that people treat as a market scoreboard.
Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA).whyThisMatters: It's famous but kinda weird — price-weighted and only 30 stocks makes it way less useful than the S&P 500 for tracking your portfolio.

DRIP.def: Dividend Reinvestment Plan — automatically uses your dividends to buy more shares of the same stock.
DRIP.whyThisMatters: Reinvesting dividends in solid companies that grow payouts is one of the most powerful compounding engines you can set up.

EBITDA.def: Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization — a rough proxy for operating cash flow.
EBITDA.whyThisMatters: It's the go-to metric for valuation multiples, but it overstates cash flow for asset-heavy businesses since it ignores real maintenance costs.

EPS (Earnings Per Share).def: A company's profit divided by how many shares exist. If a company made $100M and has 10M shares, EPS is $10.
EPS (Earnings Per Share).whyThisMatters: EPS shows if a stock's price makes sense — rising EPS from actual growth is way better than rising EPS from stock buybacks alone.

Equity.def: Ownership in a company through stock. You're buying a slice of the business and get whatever's left after debts are paid.
Equity.whyThisMatters: Stocks beat bonds long-term because equity gets all the upside once lenders get their cut — that's why they're riskier but more rewarding.

ETF (Exchange-Traded Fund).def: A basket of stocks or bonds you can buy and sell like a single stock. Instant diversification without picking individual companies.
ETF (Exchange-Traded Fund).whyThisMatters: ETFs let you invest like a pro with tiny fees — you can own the entire S&P 500 or a sector like tech with one click.

Ex-Dividend Date.def: The day you must own a stock by to get the next dividend. Buy after this date and you miss the payment.
Ex-Dividend Date.whyThisMatters: Time your purchases wrong and you'll pay for a dividend you'll never receive — the stock price typically drops by the dividend amount on this date.

Expense Ratio.def: The yearly fee a fund charges, taken as a % of your investment. A 1% expense ratio means $10 per year on every $1,000 invested.
Expense Ratio.whyThisMatters: Fees compound against you just like returns compound for you — a 1% fee can cost you 20% of your total gains over 30 years compared to a cheap index fund.

Fibonacci Retracement.def: Technical levels (23.6%, 38.2%, 61.8%) traders watch to guess where a stock might bounce or stall. Based on math patterns from the 1200s.
Fibonacci Retracement.whyThisMatters: These levels work partly because so many traders watch them — useful for setting targets or stop-losses, not for exact buy/sell triggers.

Float.def: The shares actually available for public trading, not counting what insiders and executives hold.
Float.whyThisMatters: Low float stocks can explode or crash on small orders since few shares exist — huge opportunity if you're right, huge risk if you're wrong.

Fundamental Analysis.def: Judging a stock by the actual business — revenue, profits, competitive advantages, industry trends. The opposite of just watching price charts.
Fundamental Analysis.whyThisMatters: Buying quality companies below what they're worth has crushed chart-watching strategies long-term — know what you own, not just what it costs.

Futures.def: Contracts forcing you to buy or sell something (oil, wheat, stock indexes) at a set price on a future date. Super leveraged and risky.
Futures.whyThisMatters: Companies use futures to lock in prices and hedge risk — speculators use them for massive leverage, but small moves can wipe you out fast.

GDP (Gross Domestic Product).def: The total value of everything a country produces in a year. Growing GDP means a growing economy.
GDP (Gross Domestic Product).whyThisMatters: When GDP grows faster than normal, companies make more money — when it slows, earnings disappoint and stocks struggle.

Growth Stock.def: Companies growing sales or profits way faster than average, usually expensive because everyone's betting on the future.
Growth Stock.whyThisMatters: Growth stocks live or die by expectations — they rocket when they deliver but crash hard on disappointments or rising interest rates.

Hedge.def: An investment you make specifically to protect against losses somewhere else in your portfolio. Insurance for your money.
Hedge.whyThisMatters: Good hedging costs money but saves you in crashes — over-hedging kills your gains, under-hedging leaves you exposed.

Hedge Fund.def: Private investment funds for rich people that can short stocks, use leverage, and try crazy strategies regular funds can't.
Hedge Fund.whyThisMatters: Studying hedge fund strategies shows there are tons of ways to make money beyond just buying and holding — though most still underperform index funds.

High.def: The peak price a stock hit during the trading day. Shows where sellers showed up in force.
High.whyThisMatters: When a stock keeps failing to break past the same high, that level becomes resistance — breaking through it usually triggers more buying.

Index.def: A scoreboard tracking a group of stocks. The S&P 500 tracks 500 big companies, the NASDAQ tracks mostly tech.
Index.whyThisMatters: Beating the index after fees is the real test for any active investor — most pros can't do it consistently.

Index Fund.def: A fund that just buys everything in an index like the S&P 500 in the exact same proportions. Zero strategy, minimum fees.
Index Fund.whyThisMatters: Index funds beat most active managers over time because fees compound — they're the smartest default choice for most people.

Inflation.def: When everything gets more expensive over time and your money buys less. A dollar today won't buy what a dollar bought in 2000.
Inflation.whyThisMatters: A 7% return in a 4% inflation year is really only 3% — companies that can raise prices protect you better than those that can't.

Initial Public Offering (IPO).def: When a private company sells shares to the public for the first time. It's how companies like Airbnb and Uber 'went public.'
Initial Public Offering (IPO).whyThisMatters: IPOs sound exciting but usually underperform the market afterward — early private investors already captured most of the gains.

Interest Rate.def: The cost of borrowing money, set by the Federal Reserve. When rates are high, loans cost more and savings accounts pay more.
Interest Rate.whyThisMatters: Rising rates crush growth stocks and make bonds more attractive — the Fed's rate decisions move entire markets more than almost anything else.

Intrinsic Value.def: What something's actually worth based on fundamentals, not what people are currently paying for it.
Intrinsic Value.whyThisMatters: Value investing is just buying stuff below intrinsic value — the hard part is figuring out what that value actually is.

IRA (Individual Retirement Account).def: A tax-advantaged account for retirement. Traditional = tax break now, Roth = tax-free withdrawals later.
IRA (Individual Retirement Account).whyThisMatters: Tax-free compounding for decades can add hundreds of thousands to your retirement compared to regular taxable investing — max this out first.

Large Cap.def: Huge companies worth over $10 billion — think Apple, Microsoft, Amazon. More stable but slower growth.
Large Cap.whyThisMatters: Large caps are safer in downturns because they've got diverse revenue and cheap borrowing — but don't expect them to 10x like smaller companies can.

Leverage.def: Borrowing money to invest more than you actually have. Amplifies gains when you're right, amplifies losses when you're wrong.
Leverage.whyThisMatters: Leverage turns small losses into catastrophic ones — always know how much leverage you're using, including hidden leverage in options or leveraged ETFs.

Limit Order.def: An order that only executes at your specified price or better. You control the price but risk not getting filled.
Limit Order.whyThisMatters: Limit orders prevent accidentally paying way too much in volatile markets — especially crucial for thinly traded stocks.

Liquidity.def: How easily you can sell something at a fair price without moving the market. Cash is perfectly liquid, real estate isn't.
Liquidity.whyThisMatters: Illiquid investments can trap you during crashes when nobody's buying — what looks liquid in good times can become unsellable when you need it most.

Low.def: The cheapest price a stock hit during the day. Shows where buyers stepped in to defend a level.
Low.whyThisMatters: Stocks bouncing hard off their lows show buyers defending that price — stocks closing at the low show sellers still in control.

Margin.def: Borrowing money from your broker to buy more stock than you could afford with just your own cash. Risky — if the stock drops, you still owe the money.
Margin.whyThisMatters: Margin doubles your gains and your losses — and margin calls can force you to sell at the absolute worst moment.

Margin Call.def: Your broker demanding you deposit more money because your leveraged positions lost too much. Fail to respond and they'll sell your stuff.
Margin Call.whyThisMatters: Margin calls force selling at rock-bottom prices — avoid them by never using more leverage than you can cover if things go south.

Market Cap (Market Capitalization).def: The total value of a company's outstanding shares. Stock price multiplied by total shares. It's the market's price tag on the whole company.
Market Cap (Market Capitalization).whyThisMatters: Market cap tells you what the entire business costs — it's the foundation of every valuation metric and determines which indexes track the stock.

Market Correction.def: A drop of 10–20% in the market from a recent high. Normal and healthy, happens roughly once a year.
Market Correction.whyThisMatters: Corrections happen every year or two and are totally normal — staying calm and buying during them often gives you your best entry prices.

Market Order.def: An order to buy or sell immediately at whatever the current price is. Fast execution, but you don't control the price.
Market Order.whyThisMatters: Market orders guarantee you get in or out but can cost way more than you expected in fast-moving or illiquid stocks.

Maturity Date.def: When a bond officially expires and the issuer pays you back the original loan amount.
Maturity Date.whyThisMatters: Companies with tons of bonds maturing at once face refinancing pressure — if credit markets tighten, that becomes a serious problem.

Mid Cap.def: Companies worth $2–10 billion. Bigger than startups, smaller than giants. Often the sweet spot for growth.
Mid Cap.whyThisMatters: Mid caps have survived early-stage risks but still have room to grow — they can offer better returns than stable large caps without small-cap chaos.

Momentum.def: Stocks going up tend to keep going up, stocks going down tend to keep going down. Trends persist longer than you'd expect.
Momentum.whyThisMatters: Momentum is one of the most reliable patterns in finance — stocks outperforming over 6–12 months often keep outperforming for months after.

Moving Average.def: The average price of a stock over X days, updated daily. Smooths out noise to show the underlying trend.
Moving Average.whyThisMatters: Moving averages help you see if a stock's trending up or down through short-term chaos — don't use them for exact timing, use them for direction.

Mutual Fund.def: A pool of money from many investors, managed by professionals who pick stocks or bonds. You own a slice of the whole portfolio.
Mutual Fund.whyThisMatters: Mutual funds offer diversification and pro management but charge fees — most can't beat a cheap index fund after those fees.

NASDAQ.def: The second-biggest U.S. stock exchange, home to most tech companies like Apple, Amazon, and Google.
NASDAQ.whyThisMatters: NASDAQ is way more tech-heavy than the S&P 500 — when tech's hot it outperforms, when tech crashes it gets crushed.

Net Asset Value (NAV).def: What one share of a mutual fund or ETF is actually worth based on the fund's holdings. Total assets minus liabilities, divided by shares.
Net Asset Value (NAV).whyThisMatters: ETFs can trade above or below NAV — if you're buying niche or illiquid ETFs, trading far from NAV means you're overpaying or getting a deal.

Net Income.def: Total profit after paying all expenses, taxes, and interest. The famous 'bottom line' everyone talks about.
Net Income.whyThisMatters: Net income looks good on paper but can be manipulated — always check if it matches free cash flow to know if profits are real or just accounting tricks.

NYSE (New York Stock Exchange).def: The world's biggest stock exchange by total value, listing established giants like Berkshire Hathaway and JPMorgan.
NYSE (New York Stock Exchange).whyThisMatters: NYSE companies tend to be older and more stable with stricter listing rules — you'll usually find tighter spreads and deeper liquidity here.

Open Interest.def: How many options or futures contracts are currently active and haven't been closed out yet. Think of it like open bets that haven't been settled.
Open Interest.whyThisMatters: When open interest rises with prices, the trend's legit — when it drops, people are cashing out and momentum's fading.

Options.def: Contracts that let you buy or sell a stock at a locked-in price before a deadline — but you're not forced to. You're buying the choice, not the stock itself.
Options.whyThisMatters: They're powerful for betting on direction or protecting your stocks, but they lose value over time and can amplify losses fast if you don't know what you're doing.

Options Chain.def: A big table showing every call and put option available for a stock, organized by price and expiration date.
Options Chain.whyThisMatters: It shows where smart money's placing bets — high open interest spots reveal what traders expect the stock to do.

P/B Ratio (Price-to-Book).def: Compares a stock's price to the company's book value (assets minus liabilities) per share.
P/B Ratio (Price-to-Book).whyThisMatters: Under 1.0 means the market thinks the company's worth less than its assets on paper — could be a bargain or a warning the assets are trash.

P/E Ratio (TTM).def: Price-to-earnings using the last twelve months of actual earnings — backward-looking but super current.
P/E Ratio (TTM).whyThisMatters: Compare it to forward P/E to see how much growth Wall Street expects — a big gap means they're betting on earnings jumps.

Passive Investing.def: Buy index funds that track the whole market and just hold them — no stock picking, minimal fees.
Passive Investing.whyThisMatters: Decades of proof show it beats most pros after fees, so start here unless you've got serious research firepower.

Penny Stock.def: Stocks under $5 a share, usually tiny companies with sketchy financials and wild price swings.
Penny Stock.whyThisMatters: They're magnets for scams and manipulation — wide spreads and low liquidity mean you'll get wrecked even if the story sounds good.

Portfolio.def: Everything you own in investments — stocks, bonds, funds, all of it together.
Portfolio.whyThisMatters: Think portfolio-first, not just individual picks — even great stocks can wreck you if you're too concentrated or everything moves together.

Preferred Stock.def: A type of stock that gets paid dividends before common stock and has priority if the company goes bust, but usually doesn't have voting rights.
Preferred Stock.whyThisMatters: It's safer than common stock but caps your upside — matters most when analyzing shaky companies to see who actually gets paid.

Premium.def: What you pay to buy an option, or how much above face value something's trading. For options, it's intrinsic value plus time value.
Premium.whyThisMatters: That premium is your max loss when buying options and your max gain when selling them — know what you're paying for.

Price Target.def: Where an analyst thinks a stock will be in 12 months.
Price Target.whyThisMatters: Target changes move institutional money fast, but focus on why they changed it, not just the number.

Principal.def: The original chunk of money you invested or borrowed, before any gains, losses, or interest.
Principal.whyThisMatters: Protecting your starting capital through smart position sizing is how you survive long enough to compound — blow it and you're done.

Put Option.def: Gives you the right to sell a stock at a set price before expiration — it's a bet the stock drops.
Put Option.whyThisMatters: Puts let you profit from crashes or protect stocks you own — your loss is capped at what you paid, unlike shorting.

Rally.def: When prices surge after falling or staying flat for a while.
Rally.whyThisMatters: Rallies from real news (earnings beats, new contracts) last way longer than ones from short-covering or hype.

Recession.def: When the economy shrinks for two quarters in a row — GDP goes negative, unemployment rises, companies make less.
Recession.whyThisMatters: Earnings tank across the board, so getting defensive early and aggressive early in recovery has crushed over decades.

REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust).def: A company that owns rent-producing real estate and has to pay out 90% of profits as dividends.
REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust).whyThisMatters: You get real estate exposure without buying property, plus fat dividends — but ignore net income, focus on funds from operations (FFO).

Return on Equity (ROE).def: How much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money — net income divided by equity.
Return on Equity (ROE).whyThisMatters: High ROE from debt is riskier than from great margins and efficiency — break it down before assuming it's a quality business.

Revenue.def: Total money coming in from selling stuff before any costs get subtracted — the top line of the income statement.
Revenue.whyThisMatters: Without revenue growth, long-term earnings growth is almost impossible — and organic growth beats buying other companies.

Risk Tolerance.def: How much volatility and potential loss you can stomach without panicking and selling.
Risk Tolerance.whyThisMatters: If you find out during a 30% crash you can't handle it, you'll panic-sell at the bottom and lock in losses.

Roth IRA.def: A retirement account you fund with after-tax money, but all gains and withdrawals in retirement are tax-free.
Roth IRA.whyThisMatters: Tax-free compounding over 40 years can save you hundreds of thousands compared to taxable accounts — one of the best wealth tools out there.

Russell 2000.def: Index tracking 2,000 small U.S. companies — the go-to benchmark for small-cap performance.
Russell 2000.whyThisMatters: Small caps are way more sensitive to U.S. conditions and interest rates than the S&P 500, so they move differently.

S&P 500.def: Index of 500 big U.S. companies weighted by size — the main benchmark everyone compares to.
S&P 500.whyThisMatters: Beating it consistently over 10+ years is crazy hard, so it's the standard for judging any U.S. stock strategy.

Sector.def: A slice of the economy grouping similar businesses — tech, healthcare, energy, financials, etc.
Sector.whyThisMatters: Being overweight in sectors catching tailwinds (policy, rates, economy) drives a ton of outperformance.

Securities.def: Tradable financial stuff — stocks, bonds, options, futures, any financial contract you can buy or sell.
Securities.whyThisMatters: Each type has different risks and rights — knowing what you actually own is investing 101.

Shares Outstanding.def: Total number of shares that exist for a company right now, held by everyone — institutions, insiders, retail.
Shares Outstanding.whyThisMatters: Watch how this changes — buybacks boost per-share value, but executive stock comp dilutes you.

Short Selling.def: Borrow shares, sell them now, buy them back cheaper later, return them, pocket the difference.
Short Selling.whyThisMatters: Your upside is capped (stock hits zero) but losses are unlimited (stock can moon forever) — super risky asymmetry.

Small Cap.def: Companies worth roughly $300M to $2B — bigger than micro-cap, way smaller than blue chips.
Small Cap.whyThisMatters: Less analyst coverage means more mispricings, but also way less liquidity — big sellers can tank the price fast.

Spread.def: The gap between two prices — bid vs. ask when trading, or yield differences between bonds.
Spread.whyThisMatters: Tight spreads mean low risk and good liquidity; widening spreads scream stress and often predict stock volatility.

Stock.def: A piece of ownership in a company — you get a slice of profits and assets.
Stock.whyThisMatters: Over decades, owning real businesses beats almost everything because you capture earnings growth and value creation.

Stock Split.def: Company chops each share into multiple shares, lowering the price but keeping total value the same.
Stock Split.whyThisMatters: It's cosmetic but signals confidence and pulls in retail buyers — often a short-term boost.

Stop-Loss Order.def: An automatic sell order that triggers if a stock drops to a certain price — meant to cap losses.
Stop-Loss Order.whyThisMatters: Set them at real support levels (not random percentages) so you don't get shaken out by normal volatility.

Strike Price.def: The price you can buy (call) or sell (put) a stock at if you use your option.
Strike Price.whyThisMatters: Comparing strike to current price tells you if the option's already profitable (in-the-money) or just betting on future movement (out-of-the-money).

Support Level.def: A price where buyers have stepped in before and stopped the stock from falling further.
Support Level.whyThisMatters: Breaking below major support often triggers panic selling as previous buyers bail on bounces.

Technical Analysis.def: Reading charts and past price patterns to predict where a stock's headed next.
Technical Analysis.whyThisMatters: Use it for timing and exits, not picking what to buy — it helps you get better entry points on stocks you already like.

Theta.def: How much value an option loses every day just from time passing — also called time decay.
Theta.whyThisMatters: Theta kills option buyers daily — even if you're right on direction, you can lose if the stock doesn't move fast enough.

Ticker Symbol.def: The short code for a stock — like AAPL for Apple or TSLA for Tesla.
Ticker Symbol.whyThisMatters: Learn the tickers for companies you follow so you can research fast and avoid trading mistakes.

Time Value.def: The part of an option's price that comes from time left until expiration, not current profit.
Time Value.whyThisMatters: Time value melts away faster as expiration nears — brutal for buyers, great for sellers collecting premium.

Treasury Bond.def: Long-term U.S. government debt (10-30 years) — about as safe as it gets in investing.
Treasury Bond.whyThisMatters: Treasury yields set the baseline for every other asset's return — the 10-year yield is the most important number in markets.

Trend.def: The overall direction a stock's moving — up, down, or sideways over time.
Trend.whyThisMatters: Trading with the trend wins way more often than fighting it — only go against it if you've got serious conviction and a catalyst.

Value Investing.def: Buying stocks trading below what they're actually worth, waiting for the market to catch up. Buffett's whole thing.
Value Investing.whyThisMatters: Demanding a margin of safety keeps you from overpaying and has crushed the market for decades.

Value Stock.def: A stock that looks cheap compared to its earnings, book value, or cash flow. Wall Street thinks it's undervalued and could bounce back.
Value Stock.whyThisMatters: Value stocks have crushed growth stocks over decades, but watch out for value traps — some cheap stocks just keep dropping because the business is actually broken.

Vesting.def: You don't get your stock options or equity all at once. Vesting is the timeline over which you actually earn the right to keep them — usually spread over a few years.
Vesting.whyThisMatters: If you leave before you're fully vested, you lose unvested shares. Understanding your vesting schedule matters when deciding whether to stay or bounce.

Volatility.def: How wildly a stock's price swings up and down. High volatility = big price swings. Low volatility = pretty stable.
Volatility.whyThisMatters: Volatility cuts both ways — yeah it's risky, but if you can stomach the swings and buy during freakouts, you'll usually beat people who panic-sell.

Volume.def: How many shares of a stock traded hands during a certain period. Heavy volume means lots of action.
Volume.whyThisMatters: Volume confirms whether a price move is legit — a breakout on huge volume is way more reliable than one on barely any trading.

Yield.def: The income you get from an investment (like dividends or interest) as a percentage of what you paid for it.
Yield.whyThisMatters: For bonds, yield-to-maturity tells you your full return. For stocks, dividend yield is just part of the picture — you also need price appreciation to win big.

Yield Curve.def: A graph showing interest rates on bonds with different time horizons, from short-term to long-term. Normally long-term rates are higher.
Yield Curve.whyThisMatters: When the curve inverts (short-term rates higher than long-term), a recession almost always follows. It's the single most-watched recession predictor on Wall Street.

52-Week Range.def: The highest and lowest prices a stock hit over the past year.
52-Week Range.whyThisMatters: Shows you whether the stock is near its peak, basement, or somewhere in between — helps you spot whether sentiment is totally overheated or beaten down.

All-Time High (ATH).def: The highest price a stock has ever traded at, period.
All-Time High (ATH).whyThisMatters: Stocks hitting new all-time highs on solid fundamentals often keep climbing — there's no one left 'waiting to break even,' so less selling pressure holding it back.

Analyst Rating.def: Wall Street analysts slap buy, hold, or sell ratings on stocks based on their research and predictions.
Analyst Rating.whyThisMatters: About 55% of ratings are buys and under 7% are sells, so a rare sell rating actually means something. When a usually-bullish analyst says sell, listen.

Annual Report.def: A massive yearly document companies publish with all their financials, strategy updates, and management commentary. For U.S. companies, it's called a 10-K.
Annual Report.whyThisMatters: This is where serious investors do their homework — the footnotes and fine print reveal stuff that earnings headlines totally skip over.

Arbitrage.def: Buying something in one market and instantly selling it in another to pocket the price difference, theoretically risk-free.
Arbitrage.whyThisMatters: True arbitrage barely exists anymore because computers close gaps instantly, but understanding it helps you spot when related securities are mispriced relative to each other.

Averaging Down.def: Buying more shares of a stock that's dropping to lower your average cost per share.
Averaging Down.whyThisMatters: Only average down if the business is still solid and you'd buy it fresh at the new price — throwing good money after a dying company ('catching a falling knife') is how you blow up your account.

Basis Point.def: One hundredth of a percent (0.01%). Wall Street uses it to describe tiny interest rate or yield changes.
Basis Point.whyThisMatters: On big money, basis points add up fast — 50 basis points on a $100M portfolio is $500K a year. Always compare fees and yields in basis points to see the real difference.

Buyback.def: When a company buys its own shares off the market, shrinking the number of shares out there.
Buyback.whyThisMatters: Buybacks below intrinsic value are genius — they boost earnings per share and give remaining shareholders a bigger slice. But buybacks when the stock is overpriced destroy value.

Circuit Breaker.def: An automatic pause on trading when the market tanks by a certain percentage, designed to stop panic selling from spiraling.
Circuit Breaker.whyThisMatters: Created after the 1987 crash to give everyone a breather — they help prevent sell-offs from becoming total chaos where prices disconnect from reality.

Cyclical Stock.def: Stocks that ride the economy's ups and downs hard — think cars, construction, travel. They boom in good times and crater in recessions.
Cyclical Stock.whyThisMatters: Timing matters huge here — buying cyclicals early in a recovery when they look expensive (because current earnings are trash) often delivers the best returns.

Defensive Stock.def: Stocks that stay steady no matter what the economy's doing — utilities, healthcare, basic groceries. People need this stuff in booms and busts.
Defensive Stock.whyThisMatters: Defensives protect you in downturns and pay dividends, but they'll underperform when the economy's ripping. Great if you need stability or income soon.

Delisting.def: When a stock gets kicked off an exchange, usually for failing to meet requirements or because the company's going private.
Delisting.whyThisMatters: Delisting kills liquidity and forces some institutions to dump shares — if you see a delisting warning, it's a red flag worth taking seriously.

Earnings Report.def: Companies release their financial results every quarter (and annually), showing revenue, profit, cash flow — the whole picture.
Earnings Report.whyThisMatters: These are the biggest regular events for stock prices. Learning to analyze them (vs. expectations, vs. last year, vs. guidance) makes you a way better investor.

Federal Reserve (The Fed).def: America's central bank. They set interest rates, control the money supply, and try to keep the financial system stable.
Federal Reserve (The Fed).whyThisMatters: The Fed is the most powerful force in markets — their rate decisions affect everything from your mortgage to stock valuations to corporate borrowing costs.

Fiduciary.def: Someone legally required to put your financial interests first when managing your money.
Fiduciary.whyThisMatters: Working with a fiduciary (vs. a regular broker who just has to meet a 'suitability' standard) means they're legally bound to prioritize you — huge difference when picking an advisor.

Fiscal Year.def: A company's 12-month accounting period for financial reporting. Doesn't have to match the calendar year.
Fiscal Year.whyThisMatters: Some companies end their fiscal year in June, others in December — mixing them up when comparing quarterly data will give you totally misleading conclusions.

Forward P/E.def: Price-to-earnings ratio using estimated future earnings instead of past earnings.
Forward P/E.whyThisMatters: It's forward-looking but relies on analyst guesses — if a company's margins are about to explode, forward P/E might show it's cheap even when trailing P/E looks expensive.

Free Cash Flow.def: Cash the business generates after covering operations and capital spending. This is truly free money the company can use however it wants.
Free Cash Flow.whyThisMatters: Arguably the most important number — FCF funds dividends, buybacks, debt paydown, and acquisitions. High FCF yield is often the best sign a stock's undervalued.

Gross Profit.def: Revenue minus the direct costs of making your product. What's left before you pay for everything else like salaries, marketing, and rent.
Gross Profit.whyThisMatters: If gross profit doesn't cover overhead, the company will never be profitable no matter how well they cut costs — gross margin is make-or-break.

Growth Rate.def: How fast a company's financials (revenue, earnings, users) are increasing, shown as a percentage.
Growth Rate.whyThisMatters: Growth rate determines how quickly value compounds — 10% vs. 20% annual growth creates a massive difference over a decade, which is why investors pay up for high-growth companies.

Implied Volatility.def: The market's guess about how much a stock will move in the future, baked into options prices.
Implied Volatility.whyThisMatters: High implied volatility makes options expensive to buy but juicy to sell — pros often sell options when IV spikes during freakouts to collect fat premiums.

In the Money.def: An option that would make you money if you used it right now. For calls, stock price is above your strike price. For puts, it's below.
In the Money.whyThisMatters: In-the-money options act more like owning the stock itself and are less affected by time decay — better for investing than gambling.

Insider Trading.def: Trading stock based on secret, non-public info. Super illegal and can land you in prison.
Insider Trading.whyThisMatters: Illegal insider trading ruins market fairness, but legal insider buying (execs buying their own stock) is public info and one of the most bullish signals you can find.

Institutional Investor.def: Big organizations like pension funds, mutual funds, and endowments that invest massive amounts of money on behalf of others.
Institutional Investor.whyThisMatters: Institutions control most of the market's volume — tracking where they're putting money (via 13F filings) shows you where the smart money is moving.

Leverage Ratio.def: How much debt a company's using compared to its equity. High leverage = high risk and high potential reward.
Leverage Ratio.whyThisMatters: Leverage amplifies everything — profits in good times, but catastrophic losses when things go south. Check this before investing in any debt-heavy business.

Liquidity Risk.def: The risk you won't be able to sell an investment quickly at a fair price because there aren't enough buyers.
Liquidity Risk.whyThisMatters: Liquidity vanishes exactly when you need it most — during crashes, even normally liquid stocks can gap down hard before anyone steps up to buy.

Long Position.def: Owning a stock because you think it'll go up. The normal way most people invest.
Long Position.whyThisMatters: Long positions have asymmetric payoffs — you can only lose 100%, but gains are theoretically unlimited, which is why stocks are the best long-term wealth builder.

Margin of Safety.def: The gap between what a stock is actually worth and what you're paying for it. Your buffer against screwing up the analysis.
Margin of Safety.whyThisMatters: Benjamin Graham's core principle — buying at 50 cents on the dollar means you can be way off in your valuation and still not lose money.

Market Maker.def: Firms or people who constantly quote buy and sell prices for a stock, making it easier for others to trade by always being ready to transact.
Market Maker.whyThisMatters: Market makers earn the bid-ask spread for providing liquidity — when they pull back during volatility, it becomes way harder (and more expensive) to trade.

Market Sentiment.def: The overall vibe investors have toward a stock or the market — bullish, bearish, or somewhere in between.
Market Sentiment.whyThisMatters: Sentiment drives short-term moves — extreme fear or greed usually creates the best opportunities if you can keep your head while everyone else is losing theirs.

Operating Income.def: Profit from the actual business after paying for operations, before interest and taxes. Also called EBIT.
Operating Income.whyThisMatters: Operating income shows how profitable the core business is, isolated from financing choices and tax stuff — the purest measure of business performance.

Out of the Money.def: An option with no current value — a call where the stock is below your strike, or a put where it's above.
Out of the Money.whyThisMatters: OTM options are cheap but expire worthless most of the time — you need a big move in your direction before expiration, making them high-risk lottery tickets.

Overvalued.def: When a stock's price is higher than what the fundamentals say it's actually worth.
Overvalued.whyThisMatters: Even great companies can be bad investments if you overpay — the price drop from multiple compression alone can wipe out years of earnings growth.

Paper Trading.def: Practicing trading with fake money to test strategies and build skills without risking actual cash.
Paper Trading.whyThisMatters: Paper trading teaches mechanics and tests ideas risk-free, but it doesn't replicate the gut-punch of real losses — succeeding with fake money doesn't guarantee real success.

Payout Ratio.def: Shows what percentage of a company's profits get paid out as dividends. If a company earns $1 per share and pays $0.60 in dividends, that's a 60% payout ratio.
Payout Ratio.whyThisMatters: Above 80-90% means the dividend could be in trouble if earnings drop — below 50% means there's room to grow it, which is what you want if you're investing for income.

Portfolio Rebalancing.def: When your winners grow too big, you sell some and buy more of what's lagging to get back to your target mix. Sounds backwards, but it works.
Portfolio Rebalancing.whyThisMatters: It forces you to buy low and sell high on autopilot — do it once a year and you'll consistently beat people who just let their portfolios drift.

Quantitative Easing (QE).def: When the Federal Reserve buys tons of bonds to pump money into the economy and push interest rates down. Think of it as the ultimate stimulus package.
Quantitative Easing (QE).whyThisMatters: QE made everything go up from 2008-2021 — stocks, crypto, housing. When they reverse it (quantitative tightening), everything hurts.

Resistance Level.def: A price where a stock keeps getting rejected and can't break through. It's like an invisible ceiling formed by sellers who've been waiting to get their money back.
Resistance Level.whyThisMatters: Old highs become resistance because everyone who bought at the top wants to sell at breakeven — know where these levels are before you set your price targets.

Retail Investor.def: Just regular people (like you) buying stocks with their own money, not institutions or professionals.
Retail Investor.whyThisMatters: Retail investors can actually move markets now — GameStop proved that coordinated buying creates wild opportunities and risks when everyone piles in at once.

Risk-Reward Ratio.def: Compares how much you could lose versus how much you could make on a trade. A 1:3 ratio means risking $100 to potentially make $300.
Risk-Reward Ratio.whyThisMatters: You can be wrong half the time and still crush it with 1:3 risk/reward — good traders obsess over this before entering any position.

Sector Rotation.def: Moving your money between different industries based on where we are in the economic cycle. Tech crushes in expansions, utilities hold up in recessions.
Sector Rotation.whyThisMatters: Different sectors dominate at different times — energy and materials lead early recoveries while defensive stocks outperform when things slow down.

Short Interest.def: The total number of shares that investors have borrowed and sold, betting the price will drop.
Short Interest.whyThisMatters: High short interest can mean either everyone's right to be bearish OR you're one catalyst away from a short squeeze that sends the stock parabolic.

Short Squeeze.def: When a stock rips higher and forces short sellers to panic-buy shares to cut their losses, which pushes the price even higher. It's a feedback loop.
Short Squeeze.whyThisMatters: GameStop went from $20 to $483 in three weeks because of this — short squeezes are explosive but almost never sustainable.

Speculation.def: Buying something purely because you think the price will go up soon, not because of its actual value. You're betting on momentum and hype.
Speculation.whyThisMatters: Speculation isn't investing — you need tight stop losses and small position sizes because there's no fundamental floor under the price.

Stagflation.def: The economic nightmare where growth stalls out but prices keep rising anyway. High inflation plus high unemployment at the same time.
Stagflation.whyThisMatters: Stagflation is brutal because there's no good fix — raise rates to fight inflation and you kill growth, stimulate growth and you make inflation worse. The '70s destroyed portfolios.

Standard Deviation.def: Measures how much an investment's returns bounce around its average. Higher standard deviation means wilder swings up and down.
Standard Deviation.whyThisMatters: It's the standard measure of portfolio risk, but it counts upside volatility as 'risky' too — most people only care about downside, which is why max drawdown matters more.

Trailing P/E.def: Price-to-earnings ratio using actual reported profits from the last 12 months, not estimates. It's what already happened, not what might happen.
Trailing P/E.whyThisMatters: Trailing P/E uses real numbers — compare it to forward P/E to see how much earnings growth is already baked into the current price.

Undervalued.def: When a stock's trading below what it's actually worth based on fundamentals. The market hasn't recognized its true value yet.
Undervalued.whyThisMatters: Finding truly undervalued stocks is the goal of value investing — but you need to make sure it's not just cheap because the business is dying.

Unrealized Gain/Loss.def: Profit or loss on paper from investments you still own. The stock went up but you haven't sold, so it's not 'real' yet.
Unrealized Gain/Loss.whyThisMatters: Unrealized gains don't trigger taxes until you sell — this is why buy-and-hold is so powerful, your gains compound tax-free as long as you don't sell.

Venture Capital.def: Money invested in early-stage startups with huge growth potential in exchange for equity. High risk, potentially massive returns.
Venture Capital.whyThisMatters: VC is how every tech giant got funded — understanding dilution and liquidation preferences is key if you're ever evaluating startup opportunities.

Vix (Volatility Index).def: The CBOE Volatility Index, often called the 'fear gauge,' measuring expected 30-day volatility in the S&P 500.
Vix (Volatility Index).whyThisMatters: The VIX is the market's fear meter — VIX above 30 typically signals extreme fear and often marks near-term market bottoms, while VIX below 15 signals complacency that often precedes corrections.

Warrant.def: Gives you the right to buy a company's stock at a set price before a certain date, kind of like a long-term call option that the company itself issued.
Warrant.whyThisMatters: Warrants offer leveraged upside but dilute shareholders when exercised — SPAC warrants became a huge speculation vehicle in 2020-2021.

Watchlist.def: Your personal list of stocks you're tracking and might buy when the price is right.
Watchlist.whyThisMatters: The best opportunities flash by fast during market crashes — having a prepared watchlist means you can act decisively when everyone else is frozen.

Working Capital.def: Current assets minus current liabilities. Basically the cash and liquid assets available to run the business day-to-day.
Working Capital.whyThisMatters: Declining working capital despite growing revenue means the company's burning cash faster than it's making it — major red flag.

52-Week Change.def: How much a stock's price has changed over the past year, shown as a percentage.
52-Week Change.whyThisMatters: Stocks outperforming the index over 52 weeks tend to keep outperforming short-term — it's one of the most reliable momentum signals.

Avg Volume.def: The average number of shares traded each day, usually calculated over the last 30-90 days.
Avg Volume.whyThisMatters: Low volume means you'll struggle to get in or out without moving the price yourself — anything under 100k shares daily is risky for bigger positions.

Forward Dividend.def: The projected dividend per share over the next 12 months based on the most recent payment annualized.
Forward Dividend.whyThisMatters: Compare it to free cash flow and payout ratio to see if the dividend is safe — you want dividends covered by at least 1.2-1.5x free cash flow.

Ex-Date.def: The first day a stock trades without the right to the next dividend. Buy before this date to get paid, after it and you miss out.
Ex-Date.whyThisMatters: The stock price drops by roughly the dividend amount on ex-date — there's no free money in buying the day before just to collect the dividend.

1y Target Est.def: The consensus one-year price target from analyst estimates.
1y Target Est.whyThisMatters: Same as 1Y Target Est — treat this as a sentiment gauge rather than a reliable valuation, since analysts frequently anchor targets to recent price levels.

Beta (5Y Monthly).def: Measures how volatile a stock is compared to the overall market using five years of monthly data. Beta of 1 = moves with the market, above 1 = more volatile, below 1 = less.
Beta (5Y Monthly).whyThisMatters: Five-year beta is more reliable than short-term calculations — it shows long-term risk characteristics instead of just recent volatility.

Market Open.def: 9:30 AM Eastern, Monday through Friday (except holidays) — when the stock market starts trading.
Market Open.whyThisMatters: The first 10-30 minutes are chaotic as overnight orders clear — pros usually wait for opening volatility to settle before making moves.

Market Close.def: 4:00 PM Eastern — when the stock market stops regular trading and closing prices become official.
Market Close.whyThisMatters: The last 30 minutes see huge volume from institutional rebalancing — closing prices are what everyone uses to calculate daily performance.

After Hours.def: Trading that happens after 4:00 PM when the market's officially closed. Lower volume, wider spreads, bigger price swings.
After Hours.whyThisMatters: After-hours moves on earnings can be wild but often reverse during regular hours — wait for normal market confirmation before acting.

Pre-Market.def: Trading before the 9:30 AM market open, usually starting around 4:00 AM Eastern. Thinly traded with exaggerated moves.
Pre-Market.whyThisMatters: Pre-market shows early sentiment on news but prices often shift significantly once the full market opens — treat it as a signal, not a conclusion.

Previous Close.def: The last official price from the previous trading session — it's the baseline for calculating today's change.
Previous Close.whyThisMatters: Comparing pre-market or after-hours prices to previous close instantly shows how much sentiment shifted overnight.

Open.def: The first price a stock trades at when the market opens at 9:30 AM.
Open.whyThisMatters: Comparing the open to yesterday's close reveals overnight sentiment shifts — gaps up or down set the tone for the day's action.

Bid.def: The highest price someone's willing to pay to buy a stock right now.
Bid.whyThisMatters: The bid is what you actually get when selling — in thin markets, it can be way below the last trade price, meaning you'll take a haircut to exit fast.

Ask.def: The lowest price someone's willing to accept to sell a stock right now.
Ask.whyThisMatters: The ask is what you actually pay when buying — in illiquid stocks, the gap between bid and ask can cost you serious money on every trade.

Bid Size.def: How many shares buyers are willing to purchase at the current bid price.
Bid Size.whyThisMatters: Small bid size means selling pressure drops the price fast — in thinly traded stocks, a big sell order cascades through multiple bid levels.

Ask Size.def: How many shares are available to buy at the current ask price.
Ask Size.whyThisMatters: Small ask size means a large buy order will spike the price — if you're building a big position, thin ask depth means you'll move the market against yourself.

Day's Range.def: The spread between today's lowest and highest prices. Shows how much the stock bounced around during the session.
Day's Range.whyThisMatters: Seeing where the current price sits in today's range tells you if buyers or sellers have momentum right now.

52 Week Range.def: The gap between the highest and lowest prices over the last year. Shows the stock's recent extremes.
52 Week Range.whyThisMatters: Where the current price sits in the 52-week range gives instant context — near the high signals strength, near the low might be opportunity or trouble.

Avg. Volume.def: The average number of shares traded daily, typically measured over 30, 60, or 90 days.
Avg. Volume.whyThisMatters: Liquidity only matters when you need to sell — average volume tells you how quickly you can exit without crushing the price.

Market Cap.def: The total value of all a company's shares: stock price times total shares outstanding. Tells you how big the company is.
Market Cap.whyThisMatters: Market cap is the first thing to check — it determines index inclusion, institutional interest, and sets the context for every other valuation metric.

Market Cap (intraday).def: What the whole company's worth right now — stock price × total shares, changing every second the market's open.
Market Cap (intraday).whyThisMatters: Intraday numbers jump around constantly, so use closing prices when you're actually calculating P/E or comparing valuations.

PE Ratio (TTM).def: Price-to-earnings using the last 12 months of actual profit instead of old annual numbers.
PE Ratio (TTM).whyThisMatters: TTM P/E shows what's happening right now — compare it to forward P/E to see how much future growth Wall Street's already betting on.

EPS (TTM).def: Earnings per share over the past year, rolling forward every quarter to stay current.
EPS (TTM).whyThisMatters: This catches momentum companies mid-year when annual numbers are already outdated and tells you what earnings power looks like today.

Earnings Date.def: When the company drops its quarterly or yearly financial results.
Earnings Date.whyThisMatters: Stocks swing hard around earnings — traders play options for the move, smart long-term investors wait for post-earnings chaos to buy or sell.

Forward Dividend & Yield.def: Shows both the expected annual dividend payment and what percentage return that is at the current stock price.
Forward Dividend & Yield.whyThisMatters: Lets you instantly compare income stocks — a $5 dividend means nothing without knowing if that's 2% or 8% of your investment.

1Y Target Est.def: Wall Street analysts' average guess for where the stock will be in a year.
1Y Target Est.whyThisMatters: Use it as a sentiment check, not gospel — analysts are slow to update and their targets tend to just follow whatever the stock already did.

PEG Ratio.def: P/E divided by growth rate — trying to figure out if you're paying too much for growth.
PEG Ratio.whyThisMatters: Under 1.0 might mean the stock's cheap for its growth, but growth estimates are super unreliable so only compare within the same industry.

PEG Ratio (5yr expected).def: P/E split by the estimated 5-year growth rate instead of just next year's guess.
PEG Ratio (5yr expected).whyThisMatters: Smooths out short-term noise — really helpful for cyclical companies where next year's earnings might be distorted by where we are in the cycle.

Price/Sales.def: Market cap divided by total revenue — how much you're paying per dollar of sales.
Price/Sales.whyThisMatters: Works when earnings are negative or temporarily crushed, but anything above 20x means you're betting on insane growth and margins.

Price/Sales (ttm).def: P/S using the trailing 12 months of revenue for the most up-to-date picture.
Price/Sales (ttm).whyThisMatters: Catches fast-growers where revenue's jumping every quarter and last year's annual number is already ancient history.

Price/Book.def: Stock price compared to the book value of equity per share — what's on the balance sheet.
Price/Book.whyThisMatters: Below 1.0 in a profitable business is classic value territory — the market's pricing it under asset value, which can be a steal if it's actually earning decent returns.

Price/Book (mrq).def: P/B ratio using this quarter's book value instead of stale annual data.
Price/Book (mrq).whyThisMatters: Critical for banks and financials where book value swings fast with mark-to-market changes and loan adjustments.

Enterprise Value.def: What you'd actually pay to own the entire company — market cap plus all debt minus cash.
Enterprise Value.whyThisMatters: EV lets you compare companies fairly even when one's loaded with debt and another's sitting on cash — you're valuing the whole business, not just equity.

Enterprise Value/Revenue.def: Enterprise value divided by sales — used when there's no profit yet to anchor valuation.
Enterprise Value/Revenue.whyThisMatters: The main metric for SaaS and growth tech that aren't profitable — but you need to check growth rate and margins to know if a high multiple makes sense.

Enterprise Value/EBITDA.def: EV divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization — M&A's favorite multiple.
Enterprise Value/EBITDA.whyThisMatters: This is what private equity and acquirers look at first — compare to historical and peer averages to see if the company's cheap or expensive relative to takeover values.

EV/Revenue.def: Enterprise value over sales — the valuation anchor when earnings don't exist or are negative.
EV/Revenue.whyThisMatters: Your only option for high-growth unprofitable companies — at least revenue is a real, audited number when there's no earnings to build a P/E from.

EV/EBITDA.def: Enterprise value over EBITDA — the most common comparable across companies with different debt loads.
EV/EBITDA.whyThisMatters: Strips out capital structure and depreciation differences so you can actually compare companies apples-to-apples across leverage levels.

Fiscal Year Ends.def: The date a company's financial year wraps up and triggers annual reports.
Fiscal Year Ends.whyThisMatters: Not everyone reports in December — knowing fiscal year-ends keeps you on top of earnings schedules and lets you compare companies on matching time periods.

Most Recent Quarter.def: The latest three-month period the company's reported results for.
Most Recent Quarter.whyThisMatters: For fast-changing companies, the gap between annual and MRQ data can be huge — always use MRQ when things are moving quickly.

MRQ.def: Short for Most Recent Quarter — the freshest three months of financial data available.
MRQ.whyThisMatters: MRQ gives you the latest snapshot of what's actually happening right now instead of relying on year-old annual figures.

Profit Margin.def: Net income as a percentage of revenue — the bottom line of how much profit survives after everything.
Profit Margin.whyThisMatters: High margins mean pricing power and a quality business, low margins mean you're one bad quarter away from losses and need perfect execution just to stay profitable.

Operating Margin.def: Operating profit as a percent of sales — profitability from the core business before interest and taxes.
Operating Margin.whyThisMatters: The single most important profitability metric for most companies — rising operating margins mean the business is getting more efficient or has pricing power.

Operating Margin (ttm).def: Operating margin over the last 12 months, updated every quarter.
Operating Margin (ttm).whyThisMatters: TTM gives you the freshest efficiency picture — track the trend to see if margins are expanding, flat, or getting squeezed.

Return on Assets.def: Net income divided by total assets — tells you how well management turns assets into profit.
Return on Assets.whyThisMatters: Huge for asset-heavy industries like banks and manufacturing — consistently beating peers on ROA means better management and smarter capital allocation.

Return on Assets (ttm).def: ROA using the trailing 12 months of income for the most current efficiency read.
Return on Assets (ttm).whyThisMatters: TTM ROA shows if the company's getting better or worse at squeezing profit from its asset base right now.

Return on Equity.def: Net income divided by shareholders' equity — the return generated on every dollar shareholders have invested.
Return on Equity.whyThisMatters: Above 15% consistently is the mark of an elite business — but check if it's real operational strength or just debt leverage using the DuPont breakdown.

Return on Equity (ttm).def: ROE over the past 12 months, giving you the current performance snapshot.
Return on Equity (ttm).whyThisMatters: TTM ROE catches turnarounds or deteriorations way faster than waiting for annual figures — critical when business conditions are shifting fast.

ROE.def: Return on Equity — how much profit the company generates per dollar of shareholder money invested.
ROE.whyThisMatters: Buffett's go-to metric — sustained high ROE (15%+) without crazy leverage means the company has real competitive advantages that let it earn exceptional returns.

ROA.def: Return on Assets — profit generated per dollar of assets the company owns.
ROA.whyThisMatters: Shows asset efficiency — software companies naturally crush manufacturers here, so compare within industries and watch for rising ROA as a sign of improving capital deployment.

ROIC.def: Return on Invested Capital — operating profit after tax divided by all the capital deployed in the business.
ROIC.whyThisMatters: ROIC above your cost of capital means the business creates value — high-ROIC companies compound wealth way faster even at the same growth rate as low-ROIC peers.

Revenue (ttm).def: Total sales over the past 12 months, rolling forward every quarter.
Revenue (ttm).whyThisMatters: TTM revenue is your current annual run rate — use this for P/S ratios and growth analysis instead of outdated fiscal year numbers.

Revenue Per Share (ttm).def: TTM revenue divided by diluted shares — sales on a per-share basis.
Revenue Per Share (ttm).whyThisMatters: Lets you track top-line growth even when share count changes from buybacks or dilution — keeps your comparisons consistent over time.

Quarterly Revenue Growth (yoy).def: How much revenue grew this quarter versus the same quarter last year, in percentage terms.
Quarterly Revenue Growth (yoy).whyThisMatters: Revenue growth is what drives long-term value — sustained above-market growth while holding or expanding margins is how the best businesses compound.

Gross Profit (ttm).def: Total gross profit over the trailing 12 months — revenue minus cost of goods sold.
Gross Profit (ttm).whyThisMatters: TTM gross profit shows current margin strength before operating costs — track the trend to see if the core product is gaining or losing pricing power.

Net Income Avi to Common (ttm).def: Trailing 12-month net income actually available to common shareholders after preferred dividends.
Net Income Avi to Common (ttm).whyThisMatters: This is what drives EPS — if there are preferred dividends, this can be way lower than headline net income and changes the real per-share earnings power.

Diluted EPS (ttm).def: Diluted earnings per share over the past year, accounting for all potential shares from options and convertibles.
Diluted EPS (ttm).whyThisMatters: TTM diluted EPS gives you the current annual earnings picture — way more useful than last fiscal year's number if the company's on a growth tear.

Quarterly Earnings Growth (yoy).def: Percentage change in EPS this quarter versus the same quarter a year ago.
Quarterly Earnings Growth (yoy).whyThisMatters: Year-over-year kills seasonal noise and shows real momentum — accelerating YoY growth is one of the strongest signals that fundamentals are improving.

Total Cash (mrq).def: All cash and liquid investments sitting on the balance sheet as of last quarter.
Total Cash (mrq).whyThisMatters: Cash is optionality and safety — big cash relative to market cap means downside protection and the ability to fund growth, buybacks, or deals without borrowing.

Total Cash Per Share (mrq).def: Cash and equivalents divided by total shares outstanding.
Total Cash Per Share (mrq).whyThisMatters: Subtract cash per share from stock price to see what you're paying for the actual business — stocks near cash per share have massive downside protection.

Total Debt (mrq).def: All short-term and long-term debt owed as of the most recent quarter.
Total Debt (mrq).whyThisMatters: Total debt kicks off your leverage analysis — pair it with EBITDA and interest coverage to see if the debt load is manageable or dangerous.

Total Debt/Equity (mrq).def: How much debt a company has compared to shareholder equity, using last quarter's numbers. Higher ratio = more debt, more risk.
Total Debt/Equity (mrq).whyThisMatters: Last quarter's data catches companies mid-deleveraging or mid-acquisition — the annual report might already be outdated.

Current Ratio (mrq).def: Can the company pay its short-term bills? It's current assets divided by current liabilities from the most recent quarter.
Current Ratio (mrq).whyThisMatters: Year-end data can be stale — if a company's burning cash fast, you need the freshest numbers to know if it's still solvent.

Book Value Per Share (mrq).def: What each share is 'worth' on paper based on the company's accounting value, using the latest quarter's balance sheet.
Book Value Per Share (mrq).whyThisMatters: Recent data matters for companies rebuilding their balance sheet or buying back shares — last year's number might not reflect reality anymore.

Book Value Per Share.def: Take all the company's equity, divide by shares outstanding. That's what you'd theoretically get per share if they liquidated everything at book value.
Book Value Per Share.whyThisMatters: Banks and financial stocks get valued on price-to-book because their assets are mostly loans and securities that actually trade near book value.

Levered Free Cash Flow (ttm).def: The cash left over after paying all the bills including interest on debt. This is what shareholders actually get.
Levered Free Cash Flow (ttm).whyThisMatters: This is the real money available for dividends and buybacks — unlevered FCF ignores debt payments, which... aren't optional.

Operating Cash Flow (ttm).def: Cash the business actually generated from operations over the last year. The most honest health check there is.
Operating Cash Flow (ttm).whyThisMatters: Compare it to net income to see if earnings are real or accounting magic, and subtract capex to get free cash flow.

S&P500 52-Week Change.def: How much the S&P 500 moved over the past year, in percentage terms.
S&P500 52-Week Change.whyThisMatters: Compare your stock's return to this — if you're underperforming the index consistently, you need to ask why you own it.

52 Week High.def: The peak price the stock hit anytime in the last year.
52 Week High.whyThisMatters: Stocks breaking to new highs often keep climbing due to momentum — but buying right at the high takes guts and good reason.

52 Week Low.def: The cheapest price the stock traded at over the past 52 weeks.
52 Week Low.whyThisMatters: Near a 52-week low could mean opportunity or disaster — figure out if it's temporary bad news or permanent business decline.

50-Day Moving Average.def: Average closing price over the last 50 trading days. Smooths out daily noise to show the medium-term trend.
50-Day Moving Average.whyThisMatters: When the 50-day crosses above the 200-day (golden cross), traders see it as bullish. Below (death cross) = bearish.

200-Day Moving Average.def: The stock's average price over the last 200 trading days — the go-to line for long-term trend direction.
200-Day Moving Average.whyThisMatters: Most institutional traders use this as a baseline — above it is bullish territory, below it is bearish. Simple but widely followed.

Avg Vol (3 month).def: How many shares trade hands on a typical day, averaged over three months.
Avg Vol (3 month).whyThisMatters: Compare today's volume to this baseline — huge volume on a big move means conviction, low volume means it might not stick.

Avg Vol (10 day).def: Average daily trading volume over the past two weeks.
Avg Vol (10 day).whyThisMatters: Shows whether trading activity is heating up or cooling down right now — spikes above this suggest something's happening.

Implied Shares Outstanding.def: Total shares if every stock option, convertible bond, and warrant got converted. The fully loaded share count.
Implied Shares Outstanding.whyThisMatters: For startups with tons of options, this can be way higher than basic shares — it's the real denominator for EPS calculations.

% Held by Insiders.def: How much of the company execs and directors personally own.
% Held by Insiders.whyThisMatters: High insider ownership (10%+) means they eat their own cooking — they're less likely to do dumb stuff that tanks the stock.

% Held by Institutions.def: What percentage of shares are owned by big players like mutual funds, pensions, and hedge funds.
% Held by Institutions.whyThisMatters: Super high institutional ownership can mean herd behavior and big swings when they all sell at once during a downturn.

Shares Short.def: How many shares people have borrowed and sold, betting the price will drop.
Shares Short.whyThisMatters: High short interest means lots of people are betting against it — but if good news hits, they'll panic-buy to cover, spiking the price.

Short Ratio.def: How many days it would take short sellers to buy back all their borrowed shares at normal trading volume.
Short Ratio.whyThisMatters: Above 5-7 days means a short squeeze could last a while — shorts can't exit fast without pushing the price way up against themselves.

Short % of Float.def: What percentage of tradable shares are currently sold short.
Short % of Float.whyThisMatters: 30% short float means nearly a third of available shares need to be bought back eventually — that's rocket fuel if sentiment flips.

Short % of Shares Outstanding.def: Percentage of all shares (including restricted ones) that are short.
Short % of Shares Outstanding.whyThisMatters: Shows total bearish positioning — high levels mean either a lot of smart money thinks it's overvalued, or a squeeze is brewing.

Shares Short (prior month).def: Last month's short interest number, used to see if bearish bets are increasing or decreasing.
Shares Short (prior month).whyThisMatters: Rising short interest = bears piling on. Falling = they're covering, possibly because they're worried or already won.

Forward Annual Dividend Rate.def: How much dividend the company expects to pay over the next year per share, based on their latest announcement.
Forward Annual Dividend Rate.whyThisMatters: This is your income forecast — compare it to last year's to see if they're raising, maintaining, or about to cut.

Forward Annual Dividend Yield.def: Expected annual dividend divided by current stock price. What percentage income you'd earn if you bought today.
Forward Annual Dividend Yield.whyThisMatters: Unusually high yield compared to peers might mean value — or it might mean the dividend's about to get slashed.

Trailing Annual Dividend Rate.def: Total dividends actually paid over the last 12 months per share.
Trailing Annual Dividend Rate.whyThisMatters: This is confirmed cash you received — but it's backwards-looking, so check if the forward rate is keeping up or dropping.

Trailing Annual Dividend Yield.def: Last year's total dividends divided by today's stock price.
Trailing Annual Dividend Yield.whyThisMatters: High trailing yield can be a trap — maybe the stock crashed because they just cut the dividend and this number hasn't caught up yet.

5 Year Average Dividend Yield.def: What the dividend yield has averaged over the past five years.
5 Year Average Dividend Yield.whyThisMatters: Current yield way above the 5-year average might mean the stock's cheap — or the dividend's in trouble. Dig deeper.

Dividend Date.def: The day the company actually sends dividend cash to shareholders who owned the stock on the record date.
Dividend Date.whyThisMatters: The real date that matters is ex-dividend (two days before record) — buy after that and you miss the upcoming payment.

Last Split Factor.def: The ratio of the most recent stock split, like 4:1 means you got 4 shares for every 1 you owned.
Last Split Factor.whyThisMatters: You need to know this to correctly interpret old price charts — otherwise a $400 stock that split 4:1 looks like it crashed to $100.

Last Split Date.def: When the company's most recent stock split happened.
Last Split Date.whyThisMatters: Always use split-adjusted prices when looking at history, or you'll think the stock tanked when it actually just split.

Earnings Estimate.def: What Wall Street analysts think the company will earn per share in an upcoming quarter or year.
Earnings Estimate.whyThisMatters: The range of estimates matters as much as the average — wide spread means analysts have no clue, tight spread means more confidence.

Revenue Estimate.def: Analyst forecasts for how much revenue the company will report next quarter or year.
Revenue Estimate.whyThisMatters: When analysts keep raising revenue estimates, demand is accelerating — when they keep cutting them, trouble's coming before it shows up in results.

EPS Estimate.def: Wall Street's prediction for upcoming earnings per share.
EPS Estimate.whyThisMatters: Stocks often move more on beating or missing this number than on the actual earnings level — it's all about expectations vs reality.

No. of Analysts.def: How many Wall Street analysts are actively covering and forecasting this stock.
No. of Analysts.whyThisMatters: Consensus from 25 analysts is way more reliable than from 2 — low coverage might mean hidden opportunity or just higher risk.

Low Estimate.def: The most pessimistic analyst forecast — the bear case number.
Low Estimate.whyThisMatters: Beating even the low estimate by a lot is huge — missing the low estimate usually triggers a selloff and estimate cuts across the board.

High Estimate.def: The most optimistic analyst's forecast for earnings or revenue.
High Estimate.whyThisMatters: If the company can't even hit the middle estimate, the bulls with high estimates will bail fast and the stock will drop hard.

Year Ago EPS.def: What the company earned per share in the same quarter last year — the baseline for measuring growth.
Year Ago EPS.whyThisMatters: Easy comps (low year-ago number) make growth look amazing. Tough comps make solid results look weak. Always check the baseline.

Year Ago Sales.def: Revenue from the same period last year, used to calculate year-over-year growth.
Year Ago Sales.whyThisMatters: During COVID, companies had insane comps — either super easy or impossibly hard — which made growth rates basically meaningless without context.

EPS Trend.def: Whether analysts are raising or lowering their earnings forecasts over time.
EPS Trend.whyThisMatters: Upward revision trends often predict outperformance before it shows up in actual results — the momentum of estimate changes matters.
