EPS Revisions.def: When analysts change their predictions for a company's earnings per share — either bumping them up or marking them down based on new info.
EPS Revisions.whyThisMatters: Stocks where analysts keep raising estimates usually keep going up, while those getting downgraded tend to keep falling — momentum in estimate changes is a real signal.

Growth Estimates.def: Analysts' predictions for how fast a company's earnings or revenue will grow over the next few quarters or years.
Growth Estimates.whyThisMatters: These drive what investors are willing to pay today — when estimates keep getting revised upward, the stock usually follows because the market reprices for better future earnings.

Current Qtr..def: The three-month period the company's in right now — what analysts expect it to report when earnings come out.
Current Qtr..whyThisMatters: Watching how estimates change during the quarter gives you advance warning if the company's likely to beat or miss expectations.

Next Qtr..def: What analysts think the company will earn in the quarter after this one.
Next Qtr..whyThisMatters: Check what management said about next quarter on the last earnings call — it'll tell you if current estimates seem realistic or way off.

Current Year.def: The full 12-month fiscal year happening right now, used when looking at analyst projections.
Current Year.whyThisMatters: Tracking how these estimates shift throughout the year shows whether Wall Street's getting more or less confident about the company.

Next Year.def: Analyst predictions for the full fiscal year after this one wraps up.
Next Year.whyThisMatters: Markets care way more about future earnings than current ones — next year's estimates drive stock prices more than what's happening right now.

Next 5 Years (per annum).def: The average yearly growth rate analysts expect over the next five years, compounded.
Next 5 Years (per annum).whyThisMatters: This number feeds into the PEG ratio (P/E divided by growth rate) to figure out if the stock's fairly priced given its long-term growth potential.

Past 5 Years (per annum).def: How fast earnings or revenue actually grew each year over the last five years, on average.
Past 5 Years (per annum).whyThisMatters: Past growth gives context, but assuming it'll continue forever usually leads to overpaying — most growth rates eventually slow down.

Sales Surprise.def: How much a company's reported revenue beat or missed what analysts were expecting, shown as a percentage.
Sales Surprise.whyThisMatters: A revenue miss even with an earnings beat (from cost cuts) is sketchy — means demand might be weakening even if profits look okay.

EPS Surprise.def: The gap between what a company actually earned per share versus what Wall Street predicted.
EPS Surprise.whyThisMatters: Surprises cause immediate price jumps or drops — but how big the reaction is tells you how much was already priced in.

Institutional Ownership.def: The chunk of shares owned by big players like mutual funds, ETFs, and hedge funds.
Institutional Ownership.whyThisMatters: High ownership means pros did their homework, but above 90% can get dangerous — if institutions panic-sell, there aren't enough buyers left.

Institutional Holders.def: The actual names of the big funds and money managers that own significant pieces of the company.
Institutional Holders.whyThisMatters: Checking 13F filings to see which smart money funds are buying or selling gives you a second opinion on your own analysis.

Mutual Fund Holders.def: Which mutual funds own the stock, usually broken down as part of institutional ownership data.
Mutual Fund Holders.whyThisMatters: When a bunch of new funds start buying or major ones bail out, it often happens before the stock moves — they're seeing something early.

Insider Ownership.def: How much of the company is owned by executives, directors, and other people who actually run it.
Insider Ownership.whyThisMatters: High insider ownership means management's wealth rises and falls with yours — their interests are aligned, which reduces shady behavior.

Insider Transactions.def: Recent stock purchases or sales by company executives and board members.
Insider Transactions.whyThisMatters: Multiple insiders buying at the same time is one of the best free signals out there — they know more than anyone about what's coming.

Calls.def: Contracts letting you buy a stock at a set price before a deadline — basically betting the price goes up.
Calls.whyThisMatters: Calls amplify gains on small moves up, but you can lose everything if the stock doesn't move enough before expiration.

Puts.def: Contracts giving you the right to sell shares at a locked-in price, betting the stock drops.
Puts.whyThisMatters: Puts cap your loss at what you paid upfront while giving big downside exposure — useful for both speculation and protecting gains you already have.

Expiration Date.def: The deadline when your options contract becomes worthless if you haven't used or sold it.
Expiration Date.whyThisMatters: Timing matters as much as direction — being right about where a stock's going but wrong about when means watching value evaporate as expiration approaches.

Last Price.def: The most recent trade price for an options contract.
Last Price.whyThisMatters: After hours or in slow trading, this can be outdated — check bid/ask quotes instead for what it's actually worth right now.

Change.def: How many dollars a stock moved from yesterday's close to now.
Change.whyThisMatters: A $1 move means totally different things for a $5 stock versus a $500 one — that's why percentage change usually matters more.

% Change.def: How much a stock's up or down from yesterday's close, shown as a percentage.
% Change.whyThisMatters: The fastest way to gauge daily performance, but always check volume too — big moves on tiny volume are usually meaningless noise.

Bid/Ask.def: The highest price someone's willing to pay (bid) and the lowest someone's willing to sell for (ask) at this moment.
Bid/Ask.whyThisMatters: The spread between these is a hidden cost you pay every trade — wide spreads in thinly-traded stocks can kill returns no matter how good your picks are.

Volume (Options).def: How many options contracts changed hands during the trading day.
Volume (Options).whyThisMatters: Unusually high volume in certain strikes can mean someone with inside info is positioning for a big move — watch for spikes compared to normal activity.

Candlestick.def: A chart style showing a stock's open, high, low, and close prices as a single bar with lines (wicks) extending from it.
Candlestick.whyThisMatters: Patterns like hammers and engulfing candles hint at shifts in supply and demand — they work best when combined with volume and fundamentals.

OHLC.def: Open, High, Low, Close — the four main price points traders care about for any time period.
OHLC.whyThisMatters: How open and close relate (direction) plus the high-low range tells you a story about whether buyers or sellers controlled the session.

Bollinger Bands.def: Lines above and below a moving average that expand and contract based on how volatile the price is.
Bollinger Bands.whyThisMatters: When price hits the upper band, it's relatively expensive (overbought); when it touches the lower band, it's cheap (oversold) given recent volatility.

RSI.def: A momentum gauge from 0 to 100 showing if recent price moves have been too fast in one direction.
RSI.whyThisMatters: Above 70 signals overbought (possible drop coming), below 30 signals oversold (possible bounce) — but strong trends can stay extreme for weeks, so don't trade it alone.

Relative Strength Index.def: Measures how fast and how much prices have changed recently to spot overbought or oversold conditions.
Relative Strength Index.whyThisMatters: When price makes new highs but RSI doesn't (divergence), it's a warning sign that momentum's fading even if the trend looks fine.

MACD.def: Tracks the relationship between two moving averages to spot momentum shifts and potential trend changes.
MACD.whyThisMatters: When the fast line crosses above the slow line, technical traders see it as confirmation to buy — it's one of the most-watched momentum signals.

Volume Indicator.def: Tools that use trading volume to confirm price trends or warn when they might reverse.
Volume Indicator.whyThisMatters: Volume shows where big money's actually buying and selling — price alone doesn't tell the full story, so volume indicators fill the gaps.

Support.def: A price level where enough buyers show up to stop the stock from falling further.
Support.whyThisMatters: These make logical spots to buy or set stop-losses — but if a major support level breaks cleanly, that's a serious bearish signal.

Resistance.def: A price where sellers keep showing up and preventing the stock from climbing higher.
Resistance.whyThisMatters: Breaking through old resistance on heavy volume is bullish — that ceiling often becomes the new floor.

Trend Line.def: A line drawn connecting recent highs or lows to visualize the stock's overall direction.
Trend Line.whyThisMatters: When a long-term trend line breaks on high volume, it's one of the clearest signals to rethink your bullish or bearish stance.

Comparison.def: A chart tool that overlays multiple stocks or indexes so you can see their performance side-by-side.
Comparison.whyThisMatters: Shows whether your stock's moving because of company news or just riding the market — outperforming the index during sector news is especially bullish.

Log Scale.def: A chart where equal distances represent equal percentage moves instead of equal dollar moves.
Log Scale.whyThisMatters: Critical for long-term charts of stocks that've grown a ton — otherwise early history gets squashed into a flat line and you can't see the actual growth pattern.

Linear Scale.def: A chart where equal vertical space represents equal dollar changes in price.
Linear Scale.whyThisMatters: Works fine for short timeframes, but totally distorts long-term charts of companies that've compounded significantly.

Industry.def: A narrower category within a sector — like Semiconductors sits inside the broader Technology sector.
Industry.whyThisMatters: You need the right peer group to judge if valuation's fair — comparing to the correct industry makes benchmarking actually useful.

Full Time Employees.def: Total number of full-time people working at the company.
Full Time Employees.whyThisMatters: Revenue per employee and profit per employee show efficiency — if headcount's growing faster than revenue, that's a red flag.

Key Executives.def: The top leadership team — CEO, CFO, COO, and other senior people calling the shots.
Key Executives.whyThisMatters: Management quality often matters more than the business model — research their track records at previous companies to see if they create or destroy value.

CEO.def: Chief Executive Officer — the top boss who sets strategy and takes ultimate responsibility for how the company performs.
CEO.whyThisMatters: CEO quality might be the single biggest factor in long-term returns — study their past decisions, capital allocation record, and how they're incentivized.

CFO.def: Chief Financial Officer — runs all the money stuff for a company (budgets, reporting, making sure they don't go broke). When a CFO suddenly quits, it's often because bad financial news is coming.
CFO.whyThisMatters: If a company's CFO bails unexpectedly, start asking questions — it's one of the biggest red flags that earnings trouble or accounting issues are about to drop.

COO.def: Chief Operating Officer — the person who makes sure the company's day-to-day operations actually run smoothly. Think: factories humming, products shipping, systems working.
COO.whyThisMatters: A great COO keeps the gears turning while the CEO dreams big — if they leave suddenly (especially at companies that make/ship physical stuff), it can mean operations are falling apart.

ESG Score.def: A grade measuring how well a company does on environmental, social, and governance stuff — like carbon emissions, worker treatment, and board independence.
ESG Score.whyThisMatters: Big institutional investors now filter out low-ESG companies, which can tank stock prices over time as huge pools of money refuse to invest.

ESG Risk Rating.def: Measures the actual financial risk a company faces from environmental disasters, social backlash, or bad governance — not just how 'good' they are.
ESG Risk Rating.whyThisMatters: ESG risks hit the bottom line for real — think carbon taxes, lawsuits from data breaches, or scandals that crater the stock price.

Environmental Score.def: The 'E' in ESG — rates how a company handles pollution, carbon emissions, energy use, and climate risks.
Environmental Score.whyThisMatters: Environmental costs are becoming real costs — carbon regulations and energy transitions can blow up company budgets in ways traditional financial metrics miss.

Social Score.def: The 'S' in ESG — grades how companies treat workers, communities, customers, and handle data privacy.
Social Score.whyThisMatters: Social blowups (data breaches, labor scandals, supply chain horrors) have wiped out billions in market cap overnight — better social practices = less explosion risk.

Governance Score.def: The 'G' in ESG — measures board quality, exec pay fairness, shareholder rights, and whether insiders are running a clean operation.
Governance Score.whyThisMatters: Bad governance breeds accounting fraud and dumb capital decisions — companies with strong governance scores have way less scandal risk.

Controversy Level.def: Tracks how much bad press and scandal a company's been involved in — scandals, lawsuits, regulatory nightmares, all of it.
Controversy Level.whyThisMatters: Companies that keep showing up in controversies usually have deeper culture problems — repeated scandals are a massive red flag worth digging into.

Adj Close.def: The stock's closing price, but adjusted for splits and dividends so you can actually compare prices across time.
Adj Close.whyThisMatters: If you're looking at historical returns and don't use adjusted close, you'll wildly understate gains — especially for dividend stocks or anything that split.

Adjusted Close.def: Closing price tweaked to account for stock splits, dividends, and other corporate moves so historical comparisons make sense.
Adjusted Close.whyThisMatters: Always use adjusted prices when calculating returns — raw prices lie if the stock split or paid dividends.

Split/Dividend.def: Stock splits chop shares into smaller pieces (doesn't change value). Dividends are cash payments to shareholders from company profits.
Split/Dividend.whyThisMatters: Splits don't create value, but they can make shares more accessible — understanding split-adjusted history keeps you from misreading price charts.

Download.def: Lets you export raw financial data (statements, charts, screener results) so you can build your own models.
Download.whyThisMatters: Building your own spreadsheet models forces you to actually understand the numbers instead of blindly trusting someone else's calculations.

Time Period.def: The time window you're looking at on a chart or for calculating metrics — daily, monthly, yearly, whatever.
Time Period.whyThisMatters: The same stock can look amazing on a 1-month chart and terrible on a 5-year chart — always check multiple timeframes to get the full story.

Market Cap (Large).def: Companies worth over $10 billion — usually stable, heavily traded, and owned by tons of institutional investors.
Market Cap (Large).whyThisMatters: Large caps are super liquid with tons of analyst coverage — they're the backbone of index funds and usually have tight bid-ask spreads.

Market Cap (Mid).def: Companies between $2B and $10B — past the startup chaos but still with real growth potential.
Market Cap (Mid).whyThisMatters: Mid-caps often offer the sweet spot: less risky than small caps, more growth left than mega-caps, and less crowded by huge institutions.

Market Cap (Small).def: Companies between $300M and $2B — riskier and less liquid, but with bigger upside if they execute.
Market Cap (Small).whyThisMatters: Small caps get way less analyst attention, so mispricings happen more often — but liquidity can dry up fast if things go south.

Market Cap (Micro).def: Companies under $300M — super speculative, thinly traded, and often ignored by Wall Street.
Market Cap (Micro).whyThisMatters: Micro-caps are where the biggest mispricings hide, but also the biggest disasters — governance is sketchy, liquidity sucks, and info is scarce.

Price/Cash Flow.def: Stock price divided by cash flow per share — shows what you're paying for the actual cash the business generates.
Price/Cash Flow.whyThisMatters: Cash flow is harder to fake than earnings — a stock that looks cheap on P/E can look expensive on P/CF if depreciation is misleading.

Price/Free Cash Flow.def: Stock price divided by free cash flow per share — the cash left over after the company pays for everything it needs to keep running.
Price/Free Cash Flow.whyThisMatters: P/FCF is the most honest valuation metric because it shows what the business actually spits out after maintaining itself.

Debt/Equity.def: Short for debt-to-equity ratio — compares how much a company borrowed vs. what shareholders own.
Debt/Equity.whyThisMatters: Watch the trend, not just the number — if D/E keeps climbing without profits improving, financial risk is piling up.

Current Ratio.def: Current assets divided by current liabilities — basically, can the company pay its bills due in the next year?
Current Ratio.whyThisMatters: Below 1.0 means liabilities exceed assets due soon — not always a crisis, but definitely worth asking how they'll cover short-term obligations.

Quick Ratio.def: Like the current ratio, but strips out inventory — only counts cash, investments, and receivables against short-term debts.
Quick Ratio.whyThisMatters: More conservative than current ratio — if inventory is hard to sell quickly, the quick ratio shows whether truly liquid assets can cover near-term debts.

Interest Coverage.def: Operating income divided by interest expense — shows how easily the company can afford its debt payments.
Interest Coverage.whyThisMatters: Below 1.5x means almost all operating income goes to interest — dangerous territory if earnings dip even slightly.

Gross Margin.def: Gross profit as a percent of revenue — how much is left after making the product but before paying for everything else.
Gross Margin.whyThisMatters: High, stable gross margins scream pricing power and efficient production — the foundation of any consistently profitable business.

Net Margin.def: Net income as a percent of revenue — the final profit left after every single expense is paid.
Net Margin.whyThisMatters: Compare it to competitors and history — trends show whether the business is getting more or less profitable over time.

Asset Turnover.def: Revenue divided by total assets — measures how efficiently a company squeezes sales out of its asset base.
Asset Turnover.whyThisMatters: High asset turnover = capital efficiency — pair it with profit margin to understand what's actually driving return on assets.

Inventory Turnover.def: Cost of goods sold divided by average inventory — how fast a company sells through its stock.
Inventory Turnover.whyThisMatters: High turnover means strong demand and tight operations — falling turnover can signal weakening sales or piling up unsold inventory.

Pre-market.def: Stock trading before the regular market opens (usually 4am–9:30am ET) — way less volume and wider spreads.
Pre-market.whyThisMatters: Pre-market prices can be misleading because liquidity is thin — don't assume pre-market moves will hold once full trading starts.

After-Hours.def: Trading after the market closes (4pm–8pm ET) — lower volume, bigger spreads, and wilder price swings.
After-Hours.whyThisMatters: After-hours is way more volatile because fewer people are trading — prices can gap hard by the next morning when institutions jump back in.

Day High.def: The highest price a stock hit during today's trading session.
Day High.whyThisMatters: If a stock keeps failing to break above its day high, it shows sellers are defending that level — short-term resistance signal.

Day Low.def: The lowest price a stock hit during today's session.
Day Low.whyThisMatters: Bouncing off the day low on volume means buyers are stepping in — can signal short-term support.

Day Range.def: The gap between today's lowest and highest price — shows how volatile the stock was.
Day Range.whyThisMatters: Wide day ranges = high volatility and uncertain sentiment — narrow ranges mean calm, orderly trading.

Tick.def: The smallest price move allowed — for most stocks, it's one cent ($0.01).
Tick.whyThisMatters: Smaller ticks mean tighter bid-ask spreads and lower trading costs — better for retail investors.

Stop Loss.def: An automatic sell order that triggers when a stock hits a set price — limits your losses if things go south.
Stop Loss.whyThisMatters: Stop losses take emotion out of cutting losses — just don't set them so tight that normal volatility kicks you out of good positions.

Stop Limit.def: Like a stop loss, but instead of selling at market price, it triggers a limit order at your chosen price.
Stop Limit.whyThisMatters: Stop limits protect against bad fills but can fail to execute if the price gaps past your limit — you stay stuck in a falling position.

Halted.def: Trading temporarily frozen, usually because of pending news, huge order imbalances, or regulatory issues.
Halted.whyThisMatters: You can't exit during a halt even if bad news is about to drop — another reason not to bet the farm on one stock.

Uptick.def: A trade that happens at a higher price than the one right before it.
Uptick.whyThisMatters: String of upticks on volume = buying pressure — can signal early momentum if it's happening at a breakout.

Downtick.def: A trade at a lower price than the previous one — opposite of an uptick.
Downtick.whyThisMatters: Downtick rules used to limit short selling — even though most are gone, tick-by-tick selling still shows short-term price pressure.

Fill.def: When your buy or sell order actually goes through at a specific price.
Fill.whyThisMatters: Partial fills can leave you with weird position sizes — in fast markets, understanding fill mechanics keeps your strategy on track.

Execution.def: The actual process of completing your buy or sell order in the market.
Execution.whyThisMatters: Execution quality affects real returns — speed, price improvement, and slippage add up to real performance differences if you trade actively.

Slippage.def: When you try to buy or sell a stock at one price, but it actually goes through at a different (usually worse) price. Happens a lot with stocks that don't trade much.
Slippage.whyThisMatters: If you're actively trading, slippage can quietly eat away more of your returns than actual trading fees — especially in stocks with low volume.

IPO.def: Initial Public Offering — when a private company sells stock to the public for the first time and starts trading on an exchange.
IPO.whyThisMatters: IPOs sound exciting, but the data's clear: most underperform the market in the years after going public because early investors already grabbed the best gains.

Secondary Offering.def: When a public company issues more shares — either to raise cash for itself or to let big shareholders (like founders or VCs) cash out.
Secondary Offering.whyThisMatters: New shares for the company? Fine, they're funding growth. But if insiders are dumping their shares? That's a red flag they don't see much upside ahead.

Reverse Split.def: A company reduces its total shares and boosts the price per share proportionally — like turning ten $1 bills into one $10 bill.
Reverse Split.whyThisMatters: Almost always a bad sign. Companies do this to avoid getting kicked off an exchange when their stock price tanks, which means deeper problems exist.

Spin-off.def: A company splits off part of its business into a separate, independent company and gives shareholders stock in both.
Spin-off.whyThisMatters: Spinoffs actually tend to beat the market over the next few years — focused management and hidden value finally get noticed once they're standalone.

Merger.def: Two companies combine into one, and shareholders from both typically get stock in the new combined company.
Merger.whyThisMatters: Most mergers wreck value for the acquiring company's shareholders because they overpay and can't integrate smoothly — check the premium and past M&A track record before buying in.

Acquisition.def: One company buys a controlling stake in another company, often paying a big premium over the current stock price.
Acquisition.whyThisMatters: The buyer usually loses — they overpay and struggle to integrate. Always question whether management's 'synergy' math actually adds up.

Tender Offer.def: A public offer to buy shares directly from shareholders at a set price, usually above the current market price.
Tender Offer.whyThisMatters: Whether it's a buyback or hostile takeover attempt, tender offers put a floor under the stock price and give you a chance to exit at a premium.

Proxy Vote.def: Your vote as a shareholder on company decisions like board elections, exec pay, and big proposals at the annual meeting.
Proxy Vote.whyThisMatters: Most people ignore these, but proxy votes are your main way to hold management accountable — especially on executive pay and board composition.

10-K.def: The big annual report every public company files with the SEC — audited financials plus tons of detailed business info.
10-K.whyThisMatters: This is the most important doc for serious research. The risk factors, MD&A section, and footnotes have details that never make it into earnings calls.

10-Q.def: Quarterly financial report filed with the SEC — same idea as the 10-K but unaudited and covering just three months.
10-Q.whyThisMatters: 10-Qs let you spot momentum shifts quarter-to-quarter before the market catches on — compare sequential quarters to see if trends are speeding up or slowing down.

8-K.def: A filing companies must submit to the SEC whenever something major happens — earnings, leadership changes, big acquisitions, etc.
8-K.whyThisMatters: Set up alerts for 8-Ks from companies you own. It's how you find out about critical news the instant it becomes public.

SEC Filing.def: Any official document a public company submits to the Securities and Exchange Commission to stay compliant with disclosure rules.
SEC Filing.whyThisMatters: SEC filings are legally accountable — press releases can spin things however they want, but filings have to meet strict disclosure standards.

Earnings Per Share.def: Net income divided by the number of shares outstanding — shows how much profit the company made per share.
Earnings Per Share.whyThisMatters: Track EPS over years, not just quarters. Consistent growth means real business improvement; growth from buybacks alone is less impressive.

EPS.def: Short for Earnings Per Share — the company's profit divided by total shares, showing per-share profitability.
EPS.whyThisMatters: EPS growth from buybacks isn't as strong as growth from actual earnings increases, since buybacks don't improve the underlying business.

P/E Ratio.def: Stock price divided by earnings per share — tells you how much you're paying for each dollar of profit.
P/E Ratio.whyThisMatters: A high P/E only makes sense if earnings are about to grow fast enough to justify it. Always need growth context.

PE Ratio.def: Price-to-earnings — compares stock price to per-share earnings to gauge if it's cheap or expensive.
PE Ratio.whyThisMatters: It's the most quoted valuation metric, but context is everything. Cyclical peaks, accounting tricks, and growth rates determine if a P/E is actually cheap.

P/S Ratio.def: Price divided by revenue per share — used to value companies that aren't profitable yet.
P/S Ratio.whyThisMatters: Helpful for fast-growing pre-profit companies, but you need to check gross margin and growth rate too. A 10x P/S means very different things depending on the business model.

P/B Ratio.def: Stock price divided by book value per share — compares market value to the company's accounting net worth.
P/B Ratio.whyThisMatters: Most useful for banks and asset-heavy businesses. A bank trading below book value could be a steal or a sign of hidden problems.

NasdaqGS.def: Nasdaq Global Select Market — the top-tier Nasdaq listing with the toughest financial and governance requirements.
NasdaqGS.whyThisMatters: It's a minor quality signal that a company meets higher standards, though all Nasdaq tiers have their own listing rules.

Key Events.def: Important dates marked on a stock chart — earnings reports, dividends, product launches, or news that moved the price.
Key Events.whyThisMatters: Big events create predictable volatility windows. Positioning before or after them based on expected outcomes is a core investing strategy.

Mountain Chart.def: A filled-in line chart that looks like a mountain range, showing stock price over time with the area below shaded.
Mountain Chart.whyThisMatters: Great for seeing long-term trends at a glance — the shaded area makes cumulative gains or losses way more obvious than a plain line.

Advanced Chart.def: An interactive chart with technical indicators, custom time frames, and comparison tools for analyzing stock prices.
Advanced Chart.whyThisMatters: Lets you quickly spot price trends, volume spikes, and support/resistance levels alongside your fundamental research.

Earnings Call.def: A quarterly conference call where company execs discuss financial results and take questions from analysts.
Earnings Call.whyThisMatters: What management says — and doesn't say — about guidance and key metrics often matters more than the actual earnings numbers.

YTD Return.def: Year-to-date return — how much an investment is up or down since January 1st of this year.
YTD Return.whyThisMatters: Compare your YTD return to a benchmark like the S&P 500 to quickly see if you're beating the market or lagging behind.

1-Year Return.def: Total percentage gain or loss over the past 12 months.
1-Year Return.whyThisMatters: One year is too short to judge a long-term investment — always check 3- and 5-year returns too to see if performance is consistent or just a lucky streak.

3-Year Return.def: Total percentage gain or loss over the past three years.
3-Year Return.whyThisMatters: Three years usually covers at least part of a market cycle — a fund with great 1-year returns but bad 3-year returns probably just got lucky recently.

5-Year Return.def: Total percentage gain or loss over the past five years.
5-Year Return.whyThisMatters: Five years is the minimum timeframe to tell if a fund manager or strategy actually has skill vs. just getting lucky.

Valuation Measures.def: A set of metrics (P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/EBITDA) used together to figure out if a stock is cheap or expensive.
Valuation Measures.whyThisMatters: Never rely on just one valuation metric — use multiple measures together to get a complete picture of whether a stock offers real value.

Financial Highlights.def: A quick summary of key numbers — revenue, earnings, margins, valuation ratios — displayed up front on brokerage platforms.
Financial Highlights.whyThisMatters: Good for initial screening, but always dig into full statements and trends before actually investing.

mrq.def: Most Recent Quarter — means the metric you're looking at uses data from the latest completed three-month period.
mrq.whyThisMatters: MRQ data is more current than annual numbers. For fast-growing or struggling companies, the gap between annual and MRQ can be huge.

ttm.def: Trailing Twelve Months — the most recent 12-month period, giving you the latest full-year snapshot of a company's performance.
ttm.whyThisMatters: TTM is more up-to-date than the last fiscal year and captures recent trends — always use TTM when available, especially mid-year.

Analyst Insights.def: Summary commentary and buy/sell recommendations from Wall Street analysts covering a company.
Analyst Insights.whyThisMatters: Shows you what the market consensus thinks — but real outperformance usually comes from having a different, well-researched view.

Analyst Price Targets.def: Where Wall Street analysts think a stock will be trading in 12 months based on their models.
Analyst Price Targets.whyThisMatters: Target revisions often move stocks more than the initial targets — upgrades signal improving conviction and attract institutional buyers.

Analyst Recommendations.def: The combined buy, hold, and sell ratings from all the analysts who cover a stock.
Analyst Recommendations.whyThisMatters: Stocks with tons of buy ratings are usually already well-owned by big investors, leaving less room for further upside from upgrades.

Strong Buy.def: An analyst's top-tier recommendation — their highest confidence that a stock will crush it.
Strong Buy.whyThisMatters: Strong buys sound great but they're everywhere (over half of ratings in some sectors), so they've lost a lot of meaning. Do your own research.

Hold.def: An analyst recommendation to neither buy more nor sell — basically, do nothing.
Hold.whyThisMatters: On Wall Street, 'hold' often means 'mildly bearish.' A downgrade from buy to hold is usually a bigger deal than it sounds.

Underperform.def: An analyst's way of saying they expect a stock to do worse than its benchmark or sector over the next year.
Underperform.whyThisMatters: 'Underperform' is Wall Street code for 'kinda bearish' — softer than a sell, often because analysts can't issue harsh ratings without hurting banking relationships.

Sell.def: An analyst's most negative recommendation — get out now.
Sell.whyThisMatters: Sell ratings are super rare (under 7% of all ratings) because they can torch banking relationships. When one appears, especially from a former bull, pay attention.

Rating Action.def: When an analyst changes their formal recommendation on a stock — upgrade, downgrade, initiation, or dropping coverage.
Rating Action.whyThisMatters: Rating changes move stocks because institutions use them in decision frameworks. Even an upgrade from sell to neutral can trigger real buying pressure.

Maintains.def: When an analyst reviews a stock and keeps their rating the same — no upgrade, no downgrade.
Maintains.whyThisMatters: A maintained rating during a wave of upgrades is actually bearish — it means this analyst isn't joining the optimism while everyone else is.

Price Action.def: How a stock's price actually moves on the chart — no fancy indicators, just raw price and volume telling you what's happening.
Price Action.whyThisMatters: Reading pure price action shows you where buyers and sellers are actually fighting, which often predicts the next move better than any technical indicator.

Lowers.def: An analyst cuts their price target, signaling they think the stock won't climb as high as they used to believe.
Lowers.whyThisMatters: When multiple analysts lower targets at once, the stock usually has more room to fall — it means expectations are being reset across the board.

Raises.def: Analysts bump up their price targets or earnings estimates, showing more confidence in the stock's future.
Raises.whyThisMatters: A wave of raises from multiple firms often brings in big institutional money as fund managers update their models and increase position sizes.

Upgrades.def: When an analyst moves a stock up a tier — like from Hold to Buy — signaling stronger conviction.
Upgrades.whyThisMatters: Upgrades from respected analysts can trigger immediate buying pressure, especially when they come with higher price targets from multiple firms at once.

Downgrades.def: An analyst drops a stock down a notch — Buy becomes Hold, Hold becomes Sell — showing declining confidence.
Downgrades.whyThisMatters: Downgrades usually lag the actual decline, so a pile of downgrades after a stock's already tanked might actually mean the worst is over.

Performance Overview.def: A snapshot showing how a stock's done over different time windows — past week, month, quarter, year, and so on.
Performance Overview.whyThisMatters: Checking performance across multiple timeframes reveals real momentum — outperforming everywhere shows strength, while only one strong period might just be a short-term bounce.

Benchmark.def: The standard you measure an investment against — like comparing your stock picks to the S&P 500 to see if you're actually beating the market.
Benchmark.whyThisMatters: Beating a weak benchmark means nothing if you're losing to the right one — always know what you're actually being compared against.

Earnings Trends.def: The direction analysts' earnings estimates have been moving lately — are they raising or lowering their predictions for the company's profits?
Earnings Trends.whyThisMatters: Rising estimate trends often lead stock price gains — when analysts keep bumping numbers higher quarter after quarter, the stock usually follows.

GAAP.def: The official accounting rulebook in the US — every public company has to report their financials using these standards.
GAAP.whyThisMatters: Always check what companies are adding back to get from GAAP to their 'adjusted' numbers — those add-backs can hide real problems.

Normalized.def: Financial numbers scrubbed clean of one-time events and weird stuff to show what the business actually earns on a regular basis.
Normalized.whyThisMatters: Normalized earnings let you value cyclical companies at their trough — they might look expensive on current earnings but cheap on normalized ones, creating great entry points.

Beat.def: The company's actual earnings or revenue came in higher than what analysts predicted.
Beat.whyThisMatters: Beats drive short-term pops, but watch the guidance — a beat with lowered future guidance ('beat and lower') often still tanks the stock.

Miss.def: The company's results fell short of what analysts expected, usually triggering an immediate selloff.
Miss.whyThisMatters: Not all misses are equal — a revenue miss is worse than a margin miss, and one with lowered guidance is worse than one with guidance maintained.

FY.def: Fiscal Year — the 12-month period a company uses for accounting, which might not match the calendar year.
FY.whyThisMatters: Comparing companies with different fiscal years can be misleading if there are seasonal patterns — a January FY vs. December FY matters more than you'd think.

Mkt Cap.def: Market Capitalization — multiply the stock price by total shares outstanding and you get the company's total market value.
Mkt Cap.whyThisMatters: Market cap is the starting point for every valuation calc and tells you the scale you're dealing with — it's the denominator in basically every ratio.

Cash Flow from Continuing Operations.def: The cash the company's actual ongoing business generates, ignoring any divisions they've sold off or shut down.
Cash Flow from Continuing Operations.whyThisMatters: This is your clean read on sustainable cash generation — especially key when companies are restructuring and have big one-time impacts from exits.

Net Income from Continuing Operations.def: Profit from the business that's still running, excluding anything from segments the company ditched or shut down.
Net Income from Continuing Operations.whyThisMatters: Strips out noise from discontinued operations so you can see the real profitability of what the company actually does now.

Net PPE Purchase and Sale.def: Cash spent on property, plant, and equipment minus any cash from selling those assets — shows net investment in physical stuff.
Net PPE Purchase and Sale.whyThisMatters: Persistently low or negative PPE investment might mean the company's underinvesting and setting itself up for future capacity problems.

Net Business Purchase and Acquisition.def: Cash spent buying other companies minus any cash from selling business units — the net cost of M&A activity.
Net Business Purchase and Acquisition.whyThisMatters: Shows how aggressively a company's doing deals — frequent large acquisitions need serious scrutiny to make sure they're actually creating value.

Net Investment Purchase and Sale.def: Cash used to buy investment securities minus proceeds from selling them — tracks how the company's managing its investment portfolio.
Net Investment Purchase and Sale.whyThisMatters: Large net purchases might mean cash is piling up in lower-return financial assets instead of being deployed into the actual business.

Net Issuance Payments of Debt.def: New debt borrowed minus debt paid back — tells you if the company's total debt load is growing or shrinking.
Net Issuance Payments of Debt.whyThisMatters: Rising debt with weak operating cash flow is a red flag — it means they're borrowing to fund operations instead of funding them from the business itself.

Net Common Stock Issuance.def: Money raised from issuing new shares minus cash spent buying back shares — the net impact of all equity transactions.
Net Common Stock Issuance.whyThisMatters: Persistent net issuance dilutes you as a shareholder — you need to see if the capital they're raising is creating more value than the dilution costs.

Cash Dividends Paid.def: The actual cash the company handed to shareholders as dividends during the period.
Cash Dividends Paid.whyThisMatters: Compare this to free cash flow to see if dividends are actually sustainable or if they're being funded by borrowing.

Proceeds from Stock Options.def: Cash that comes in when employees exercise their stock options and buy shares.
Proceeds from Stock Options.whyThisMatters: This reduces the cost of stock-based comp, but the shares issued are still dilutive — high proceeds with big share issuance means significant dilution happening.

Net Other Financing Charges.def: Random financing cash flows that don't fit cleanly into other categories — fees, penalties, or miscellaneous capital transactions.
Net Other Financing Charges.whyThisMatters: Small individually, but consistently large 'other' charges can hide real costs from debt refinancing or financial restructuring that don't show up in headlines.

Operating Revenue.def: Money earned from what the company actually does for a living — excludes random stuff like interest income or one-time asset sales.
Operating Revenue.whyThisMatters: Operating revenue growth is what matters most — non-operating income is unreliable and can't be counted on to repeat.

Total Operating Income as Reported.def: Operating income exactly as it appears in the financial statements with no adjustments — the official GAAP number.
Total Operating Income as Reported.whyThisMatters: This is your baseline — compare it to adjusted figures to see exactly what management's adding back and decide if those add-backs are legit.

Normalized Income.def: Earnings stripped of one-time hits, non-cash items, or cyclical swings to show what the business actually earns in a normal period.
Normalized Income.whyThisMatters: This is what long-term investors should value — companies consistently earning above normalized have quality businesses, those below have structural problems.

Net Interest Income.def: For banks: interest earned on loans minus interest paid on deposits — basically the core profit engine of a bank.
Net Interest Income.whyThisMatters: Rising rates usually boost bank profits by expanding net interest margins, while falling rates squeeze them.

Interest Income Non Operating.def: Interest earned on the company's cash and investments — not from the actual business operations.
Interest Income Non Operating.whyThisMatters: In high-rate environments, companies with big cash piles can earn meaningful interest income — track it separately since it's not from the core business.

Interest Expense Non Operating.def: Interest paid on the company's debt — hits the income statement as a separate line below operating income.
Interest Expense Non Operating.whyThisMatters: Rising interest expense directly cuts into net income and free cash flow — debt levels matter way more when rates are high.

Special Income Charges.def: Supposedly one-time hits or gains from unusual stuff like restructuring, lawsuits, or selling assets.
Special Income Charges.whyThisMatters: Companies that take 'one-time' charges every single year are actually hiding ongoing costs and smoothing earnings — treat them with suspicion.

Total Unusual Items.def: Everything non-recurring lumped together — all the special charges and gains in one number.
Total Unusual Items.whyThisMatters: Consistently large unusual items are a huge red flag — these companies are delivering lower-quality earnings and you need to dig into what's being excluded.

Non Current Accounts Receivable.def: Money owed to the company that won't be collected for more than a year, sitting on the balance sheet as a long-term asset.
Non Current Accounts Receivable.whyThisMatters: Large non-current receivables can signal sketchy payment arrangements or revenue recognition issues — investigate when and if that cash is actually coming in.

Non Current Prepaid Assets.def: Stuff the company paid for in advance that won't be used up within the next year.
Non Current Prepaid Assets.whyThisMatters: Large non-current prepaids mean cash tied up in long-term contracts — watch these for impairment risk if plans change.

Current Provisions.def: Money set aside now for costs the company expects to hit soon — like warranties, legal settlements, or restructuring charges.
Current Provisions.whyThisMatters: Provisions are management's guess at future costs — under-provisioning hides real liabilities, while over-provisioning creates opportunities to boost earnings later by reversing them.

Cost of Revenue.def: Everything it directly costs to make or deliver what you're selling — includes both COGS and service delivery costs.
Cost of Revenue.whyThisMatters: Track this as a percentage of revenue over time — if it's falling, the business is getting more efficient and scaling well.

Cost of Goods Sold.def: The direct costs of making the product or delivering the service — materials, labor, and production costs.
Cost of Goods Sold.whyThisMatters: COGS divided by revenue gives you gross margin — if COGS is rising faster than revenue, margins are compressing and the company can't pass costs through.

Operating Expense.def: The ongoing costs of running the business — salaries, rent, marketing, R&D — everything except what's in COGS.
Operating Expense.whyThisMatters: OpEx as a percentage of revenue is a key efficiency metric — companies that grow revenue while keeping OpEx growth lower demonstrate real operating leverage.

Total Revenue.def: Every dollar coming in from everywhere — operating revenue plus non-operating income like interest or asset sales.
Total Revenue.whyThisMatters: Compare total to operating revenue to see how much the company relies on non-recurring income — heavy reliance on asset sales or investment income means less predictable earnings.

Total Assets.def: Everything a company owns — cash, buildings, inventory, patents, you name it. Both the stuff they'll use this year and the stuff they'll use for decades.
Total Assets.whyThisMatters: Bigger isn't always better — a company with $10B in aging factories might be worse off than one with $5B in cash and tech, so look at what those assets actually are.

Total Liabilities.def: Every dollar a company owes to someone else, whether it's due next month or in ten years.
Total Liabilities.whyThisMatters: This debt gets paid before shareholders see a dime — check if it's mostly cheap long-term loans or expensive short-term bills coming due soon.

Total Equity.def: What's left over for shareholders after you subtract all the company's debts from everything it owns. The accounting version of "net worth."
Total Equity.whyThisMatters: Comparing this book value to the stock's market price tells you if investors think the company's worth way more (or less) than its balance sheet suggests.

Interest Expense.def: What the company pays lenders for the privilege of borrowing their money, taken straight out of profits every year.
Interest Expense.whyThisMatters: When interest rates jump, heavily indebted companies can see profits collapse fast — a 1% rate hike might barely touch one company but crush another.

Income Tax Expense.def: The tax bill the company records for the period, showing what they owe (or paid) to governments.
Income Tax Expense.whyThisMatters: Divide this by pre-tax income to see the real tax rate — if it's way below the official rate, the company's probably using smart (or aggressive) tax strategies.

Research and Development.def: Money spent inventing new products or improving old ones. Companies have to expense it immediately, even though the payoff might be years away.
Research and Development.whyThisMatters: Tech and pharma companies dumping 15-20% of revenue into R&D are building tomorrow's competitive edge, but it kills today's earnings numbers.

Selling General and Administrative.def: The cost of everything that isn't making the product — salespeople, marketing, executives, office rent, all the overhead.
Selling General and Administrative.whyThisMatters: If revenue grows faster than SG&A, margins expand and profits soar. If SG&A grows faster, the company's getting bloated and inefficient.

Current Assets.def: Stuff the company expects to turn into cash within a year — actual cash, money customers owe them, inventory they'll sell soon.
Current Assets.whyThisMatters: Compare this to current liabilities to see if the company can pay its bills this year without scrambling for a loan.

Current Liabilities.def: Bills and debts the company has to pay within the next year — suppliers waiting for checks, short-term loans coming due, taxes owed.
Current Liabilities.whyThisMatters: Even profitable companies can go bust if they can't cover these near-term obligations, so always check if they have enough current assets to match.

Long Term Debt.def: Loans and bonds that don't need to be repaid for more than a year.
Long Term Debt.whyThisMatters: Check when this debt comes due and what rate it's at — if a ton of cheap debt matures soon and rates have spiked, the company's interest bill is about to explode.

Retained Earnings.def: All the profit the company has ever made and kept instead of paying out to shareholders as dividends, stacked up over its entire history.
Retained Earnings.whyThisMatters: Watching this grow year after year proves the business consistently makes more than it spends, compounding value over time.

Cash and Cash Equivalents.def: Actual cash sitting in the bank plus anything that's basically cash — like ultra-short-term bonds the company could sell in a day.
Cash and Cash Equivalents.whyThisMatters: Cash means options — weather a recession, snap up a competitor, survive without begging for loans when markets freeze up.

Accounts Receivable.def: Money customers owe the company for stuff they've already bought but haven't paid for yet.
Accounts Receivable.whyThisMatters: If this grows way faster than sales, something's fishy — maybe the company's booking revenue it'll never collect, or customers can't actually pay.

Accounts Payable.def: Money the company owes suppliers for stuff it's already received but hasn't paid for yet.
Accounts Payable.whyThisMatters: A rising AP balance can mean the company's smartly delaying payments to save cash, or desperately stretching suppliers because it's broke — context matters.

Operating Cash Flow.def: Cold hard cash the business actually generated from its normal operations, ignoring anything from selling buildings or taking out loans.
Operating Cash Flow.whyThisMatters: This is the number pros care about most — it reveals if the business truly generates cash or just looks profitable on paper through accounting tricks.

Capital Expenditures.def: Cash spent buying or upgrading long-term stuff like factories, equipment, buildings, or tech infrastructure.
Capital Expenditures.whyThisMatters: Subtract capex from operating cash flow to get free cash flow — businesses that need huge ongoing capex leave less cash for shareholders.

Debt to Equity.def: Total debt divided by shareholder equity, showing how much the company's funded by borrowed money versus owner money.
Debt to Equity.whyThisMatters: High debt amplifies gains when times are good but can wipe you out fast when earnings drop — it's financial steroids with real side effects.

Price to Earnings.def: Stock price divided by earnings per share. Tells you how many years of current profit you're paying for when you buy the stock.
Price to Earnings.whyThisMatters: It's the first thing most investors check — a high P/E might mean growth expectations are baked in, or the stock's just overpriced.

Price to Book.def: Stock price divided by book value per share, comparing market value to accounting value.
Price to Book.whyThisMatters: For banks and asset-heavy companies, trading below book value might signal a bargain or hidden problems — either way, it demands a closer look.

Price to Sales.def: Market cap divided by annual revenue. Used when companies aren't profitable yet or have razor-thin margins.
Price to Sales.whyThisMatters: Always check the gross margin too — a 5x P/S software company with 80% margins might be cheaper than a 2x P/S retailer with 8% margins.

Market Capitalization.def: The total dollar value of all a company's shares, calculated by multiplying share price by total shares outstanding.
Market Capitalization.whyThisMatters: This determines if a stock is large-cap, mid-cap, or small-cap, which affects everything from volatility to which indexes it joins.

Diluted EPS.def: Earnings per share calculated assuming every stock option, warrant, and convertible security gets converted into actual shares.
Diluted EPS.whyThisMatters: Always use diluted EPS for valuation — it's the realistic number, especially at tech companies drowning in stock options that'll eventually become real shares.

Basic EPS.def: Earnings per share using only the shares that exist right now, ignoring all the stock options and convertibles floating around.
Basic EPS.whyThisMatters: This overstates the real per-share value at companies with lots of employee options — stick with diluted EPS for anything serious.

Accrual Accounting.def: Recording revenue when you earn it and expenses when you incur them, not when cash actually changes hands.
Accrual Accounting.whyThisMatters: Public companies use this, so their reported profit can look totally different from the cash they actually collected — always check the cash flow statement too.

Accrued Expenses.def: Costs the company has racked up but hasn't paid yet, sitting on the balance sheet as a liability.
Accrued Expenses.whyThisMatters: A big pile of accrued expenses means cash is about to walk out the door, even if the income statement already recorded the hit to earnings.

Accrued Revenue.def: Money the company has technically earned but hasn't actually collected yet.
Accrued Revenue.whyThisMatters: If this keeps growing faster than cash collections, the company might be counting revenue it'll never actually see — a classic red flag.

Acid-Test Ratio.def: A tougher version of the current ratio that only counts cash, investments, and receivables (no inventory) divided by current liabilities.
Acid-Test Ratio.whyThisMatters: Inventory might be impossible to sell quickly, so this shows if the company can survive a sudden cash crunch with just its most liquid assets.

Active Management.def: When a fund manager actively picks stocks trying to beat the market, rather than just buying everything in an index.
Active Management.whyThisMatters: Sounds great, but studies show most active managers underperform index funds over time — and they charge way higher fees for the privilege.

Adjusted EBITDA.def: EBITDA with a bunch of stuff management doesn't like stripped out — one-time costs, unusual expenses, whatever they decide isn't "normal."
Adjusted EBITDA.whyThisMatters: Companies use this to make earnings look better, but aggressive adjustments can hide real problems — always compare it to the official GAAP numbers.

Agency Problem.def: The conflict that happens when executives (the agents) might act in their own interest instead of shareholders' (the principals) because their incentives don't perfectly align.
Agency Problem.whyThisMatters: Explains why CEOs sometimes make decisions that pad their bonuses but hurt long-term shareholders — it's why compensation structure and board oversight actually matter.

Algorithmic Trading.def: Computer programs executing trades automatically at lightning speed based on pre-set rules and mathematical models.
Algorithmic Trading.whyThisMatters: Algos now drive most market volume, which can create sudden weird price swings and volatility — helps explain why stocks sometimes move in ways that seem irrational.

Alpha.def: The extra return you get above what you'd expect given the market risk you took. Basically, returns that aren't just from riding the market up.
Alpha.whyThisMatters: Positive alpha means a manager or strategy is actually adding value beyond just market exposure — negative means you'd have been better off in an index fund.

Alternative Investments.def: Anything that's not stocks, bonds, or cash — private equity, hedge funds, real estate, commodities, even art and collectibles.
Alternative Investments.whyThisMatters: These can diversify your portfolio since they don't always move with stocks, but they're usually less liquid, charge higher fees, and have fewer investor protections.

Amortization.def: Spreading the cost of an intangible asset like a patent or trademark over its useful life, just like depreciation but for non-physical stuff.
Amortization.whyThisMatters: It hits earnings without using any actual cash, so add it back when you're trying to figure out how much cash the business really generates.

Angel Investor.def: A wealthy individual who writes personal checks to brand-new startups in exchange for equity, usually before venture capital gets involved.
Angel Investor.whyThisMatters: Angels are often the first money a startup gets — understanding this funding stage helps you see how companies grow from garage project to IPO.

Annual Percentage Rate (APR).def: The yearly cost of a loan including interest and fees, expressed as a percentage so you can actually compare different loan offers.
Annual Percentage Rate (APR).whyThisMatters: A low monthly rate can hide a brutal APR once fees stack up — always compare APRs, not just the advertised interest rate.

Annual Percentage Yield (APY).def: The real annual return on a savings account after accounting for compound interest.
Annual Percentage Yield (APY).whyThisMatters: APY shows what you'll actually earn after interest compounds — always compare APY (not just the base rate) when choosing where to stash cash.

Annuity.def: A financial product that pays you a fixed stream of income over time, often used to create guaranteed retirement income.
Annuity.whyThisMatters: Annuities can provide peace of mind in retirement, but they often come with high fees and penalties if you need your money early — read the fine print carefully.

Appreciation.def: When an asset's value goes up over time.
Appreciation.whyThisMatters: It's one of two ways stocks make you money (the other being dividends) — growth investors bet almost entirely on appreciation rather than income.

Asset-Backed Security (ABS).def: A financial product backed by a pool of loans like car loans, credit card debt, or mortgages bundled together and sold to investors.
Asset-Backed Security (ABS).whyThisMatters: These triggered the 2008 financial crisis when the underlying loans went bad — understanding how they work helps you spot systemic risk in credit markets.

Asset Coverage Ratio.def: Shows whether a company could pay off all its debt by selling everything it owns (minus stuff like brand value and short-term bills). Below 1.0? Even liquidating everything wouldn't cover what they owe.
Asset Coverage Ratio.whyThisMatters: Banks check this before lending — if it's too low, the company's basically overleveraged and you're taking on serious risk.

Asset-Liability Management.def: Banks juggle short-term money coming in (your deposits) against long-term money going out (mortgages they've given). Mess this up and they can't pay depositors back when everyone panics.
Asset-Liability Management.whyThisMatters: This mismatch caused Silicon Valley Bank to implode in 2023 — when interest rates spiked, their long-term bonds tanked and they couldn't cover withdrawals.

At-the-Market Offering (ATM).def: Instead of dumping a ton of new shares at once, a company drip-feeds them into the market at current prices.
At-the-Market Offering (ATM).whyThisMatters: Your stake gets diluted without the usual discount — if a company does this repeatedly, it's burning cash faster than it's making it.

Auditor's Report.def: An outside accountant's stamp of approval (or warning) saying whether the company's financials are legit.
Auditor's Report.whyThisMatters: Always look for 'unqualified' opinions — anything else (especially a going-concern warning) means serious trouble ahead.

Authorized Shares.def: The max number of shares a company's legally allowed to create, written into its charter.
Authorized Shares.whyThisMatters: Big gap between authorized and outstanding shares? They could issue way more stock and tank your ownership percentage overnight.

Average Revenue Per User (ARPU).def: How much money a company makes per customer on average each month or year.
Average Revenue Per User (ARPU).whyThisMatters: Rising ARPU means they're either charging more or getting people to buy extras — both are way healthier than just adding users who don't spend much.

Backdoor Roth IRA.def: High earners can't contribute directly to a Roth IRA, so they put money in a traditional IRA (no deduction) then immediately convert it to Roth.
Backdoor Roth IRA.whyThisMatters: Once you're making good money, this loophole keeps your retirement savings growing tax-free — totally legal and worth setting up.

Balance of Payments.def: Tracks all the money flowing in and out of a country — trade, investments, everything.
Balance of Payments.whyThisMatters: A country constantly spending more abroad than it earns will see its currency weaken and debt pile up — huge for understanding macro risks.

Balloon Payment.def: You make small payments for a while, then one massive lump sum at the end of the loan.
Balloon Payment.whyThisMatters: Keeps cash costs low now but creates huge refinancing risk later — if rates spike or credit dries up, that final payment can sink a company.

Bankruptcy.def: Legal process for people or companies drowning in debt — either sell everything to pay creditors (Chapter 7) or reorganize and try again (Chapter 11).
Bankruptcy.whyThisMatters: If you own stock in a bankrupt company, you're last in line and almost always get nothing — debt holders eat first.

Basis.def: What you originally paid for an asset, which determines your taxable gain or loss when you sell.
Basis.whyThisMatters: Tracking your basis across stocks lets you harvest tax losses strategically and minimize what you owe the IRS when you cash out.

Bear Spread.def: Options play where you profit if a stock drops, but your upside and downside are both capped.
Bear Spread.whyThisMatters: Way cheaper than buying puts outright — good for betting against something without risking a ton if you're wrong.

Bid-Ask Spread.def: The gap between what buyers will pay and what sellers want — basically the invisible tax you pay every time you trade.
Bid-Ask Spread.whyThisMatters: Wide spreads on illiquid stuff quietly bleed your returns, especially if you trade a lot — always check before buying obscure stocks or options.

Black-Scholes Model.def: Math formula that prices options based on stock price, strike, time left, volatility, and interest rates.
Black-Scholes Model.whyThisMatters: Understanding how volatility moves option prices helps you spot when options are cheap or expensive relative to what the market expects.

Block Trade.def: Huge transaction of stock done privately between big institutions instead of on the open market.
Block Trade.whyThisMatters: When a major holder dumps a big block, it often means they've lost confidence — can tank the stock price fast.

Bond Duration.def: Measures how sensitive a bond's price is to interest rate moves — basically how long you're locked in waiting for cash back.
Bond Duration.whyThisMatters: Long-duration bonds got crushed in 2022 when the Fed hiked rates — the longer the duration, the worse it hurts when rates rise.

Bond Rating.def: Letter grade (AAA to D) showing how likely a bond issuer is to actually pay you back.
Bond Rating.whyThisMatters: Investment-grade (BBB- or higher) is safer; below that you're in junk territory with way higher default risk and yields to match.

Bond Yield.def: The annual return you get from a bond, either based on face value or what it's currently trading for.
Bond Yield.whyThisMatters: Yields and prices move opposite — when yields spike, bond prices tank, which affects everything from mortgage rates to stock valuations.

Book-to-Market Ratio.def: Compares a company's accounting value to its market cap — high ratio means the market's pricing it cheap relative to assets.
Book-to-Market Ratio.whyThisMatters: Stocks with high book-to-market have historically beaten the market over long periods — classic value investing signal.

Bottom-Up Investing.def: Picking stocks by analyzing individual companies deeply, ignoring whether the overall economy or sector is strong.
Bottom-Up Investing.whyThisMatters: You can find amazing businesses even in terrible industries — Buffett's whole playbook is finding great companies no matter what's happening macro.

Break-Even Point.def: The sales level where a company covers all costs but makes zero profit.
Break-Even Point.whyThisMatters: Tells you how close a company is to losing money if revenue dips — critical for startups or cyclical businesses with volatile sales.

Bridge Financing.def: Expensive short-term loans to plug cash gaps until a company lands permanent funding or revenue kicks in.
Bridge Financing.whyThisMatters: Repeated bridge rounds scream cash crisis — usually means the business model isn't working and shareholders are getting diluted fast.

Broker-Dealer.def: A firm that either executes trades for you (broker) or trades for its own profit (dealer), sometimes both.
Broker-Dealer.whyThisMatters: When your broker's also a dealer, they might route your order in ways that benefit them, not you — payment for order flow is one example.

Bull Spread.def: Options strategy where you profit from a moderate stock increase, but you cap your max gain to reduce the upfront cost.
Bull Spread.whyThisMatters: Perfect when you think a stock will go up but not moon — you pay less premium by giving up the huge home-run scenario.

Business Cycle.def: The economy's pattern of expanding, peaking, contracting, then bottoming out before doing it all over again.
Business Cycle.whyThisMatters: Different investments crush it at different cycle stages — tech and growth in expansions, utilities and bonds in downturns.

Buy-Side.def: Investment firms managing money for clients or themselves — mutual funds, hedge funds, pensions, endowments.
Buy-Side.whyThisMatters: Buy-side analysts aren't trying to sell you anything, so their research is usually more honest than sell-side banks with conflicted incentives.

Callable Bond.def: Bond where the issuer can force you to sell it back early at a set price if they want.
Callable Bond.whyThisMatters: Companies call bonds when rates drop so they can refinance cheaper — you're stuck reinvesting your cash at lower yields, which sucks.

Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR).def: Banks have to hold a certain percentage of capital against risky loans — this ratio measures whether they're meeting that requirement.
Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR).whyThisMatters: When a bank's CAR drops too low, regulators step in, cut dividends, or force asset sales — major red flag for bank stocks.

Capital Allocation.def: How a company's management decides to spend cash — reinvest in the business, buy other companies, pay dividends, buy back stock, or pay down debt.
Capital Allocation.whyThisMatters: This is where CEOs separate themselves — great allocators compound wealth for decades while bad ones destroy value with dumb acquisitions.

Capital Efficiency.def: How much revenue or profit a company squeezes out of every dollar it invests.
Capital Efficiency.whyThisMatters: Capital-efficient businesses (like software) grow without constantly needing new funding — way better than capital-hungry models that burn cash to expand.

Capital Markets.def: Where companies go to raise long-term money by selling stocks or bonds to investors.
Capital Markets.whyThisMatters: When capital markets freeze up, companies can't fund growth cheaply — directly impacts everything from hiring to expansion plans.

Capital Structure.def: The mix of debt and equity a company uses to fund itself.
Capital Structure.whyThisMatters: Heavy debt amplifies returns when things go well but can wipe you out in downturns — always check how leveraged a company is before investing.

Capitalization Rate (Cap Rate).def: Real estate metric: annual income from a property divided by its price. Basically the property's earnings yield.
Capitalization Rate (Cap Rate).whyThisMatters: Falling cap rates mean property prices are rising faster than rents — same warning sign as rising P/Es in stocks.

Carried Interest.def: The cut (usually 20%) that private equity and hedge fund managers take from profits, on top of their management fees.
Carried Interest.whyThisMatters: It's taxed as capital gains instead of income, which is controversial — also aligns manager incentives with making you money, not just collecting fees.

Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC).def: How many days it takes a company to turn inventory and expenses back into actual cash from customers.
Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC).whyThisMatters: Shorter is way better — Amazon's is negative, meaning they collect cash before paying suppliers, giving them free financing.

Cash Flow Statement.def: Shows actual cash moving in and out through operations, investments, and financing — way harder to fake than net income.
Cash Flow Statement.whyThisMatters: Compare operating cash flow to net income — big gaps usually mean the company's inflating earnings with accounting tricks.

Cash Runway.def: How long a company can survive at its current burn rate before the bank account hits zero.
Cash Runway.whyThisMatters: Under 12 months without a clear path to profit or new funding? That's a ticking time bomb for early-stage investors.

Churn Rate.def: Percentage of customers who cancel or don't renew each month or year.
Churn Rate.whyThisMatters: High churn kills growth even if you're adding tons of new customers — for subscription businesses, retention matters more than flashy user growth.

Closed-End Fund.def: Investment fund that raises money once via IPO, then trades on an exchange like a stock — no new shares issued.
Closed-End Fund.whyThisMatters: These often trade below their actual asset value — buying at a discount can be a steal, but persistent discounts might signal bad management.

Collateral.def: Stuff you pledge to a lender that they can grab and sell if you don't pay back your loan.
Collateral.whyThisMatters: Secured debt backed by real assets is way safer than unsecured — matters big time when analyzing how risky a company's debt load actually is.

Collateralized Debt Obligation (CDO).def: Banks bundle a bunch of loans (like mortgages) into one big package, slice it into pieces with different levels of risk, and sell those pieces to investors. The safer slices get paid first, the riskier ones get paid last but offer higher returns.
Collateralized Debt Obligation (CDO).whyThisMatters: CDOs blew up spectacularly in 2008 because they hid how risky subprime mortgages really were — anytime Wall Street packages complex stuff into 'safe' investments, ask what's actually inside.

Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO).def: Similar to CDOs but built from corporate loans instead of mortgages. Investment firms pool together loans to risky companies, then slice them into tranches and sell them off.
Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO).whyThisMatters: CLOs are huge buyers of corporate debt, so when they're hungry for loans, companies can borrow cheaply — when CLO demand dries up, borrowing costs spike for a ton of businesses.

Commercial Paper.def: Big companies issue IOUs that mature in less than 270 days to cover short-term expenses like payroll or inventory. It's basically corporate short-term borrowing.
Commercial Paper.whyThisMatters: When this market freezes (like in 2008), even healthy companies can suddenly run out of cash because they can't roll over their short-term debt — it's a canary in the coal mine for financial stress.

Commodity.def: Raw stuff you can buy and sell in bulk — oil, gold, wheat, copper, coffee. Basically anything that's standardized and interchangeable.
Commodity.whyThisMatters: When oil prices jump, airlines and shipping companies hurt while energy stocks soar — commodity price swings ripple through corporate earnings and show up in your grocery bill as inflation.

Comparable Company Analysis (Comps).def: You figure out what a company's worth by comparing its numbers (like price-to-earnings ratio) to similar companies that are already publicly traded.
Comparable Company Analysis (Comps).whyThisMatters: This is Wall Street's go-to method for quick valuation — it tells you if a stock's cheap or expensive compared to its peers, which is often your first clue on where to dig deeper.

Competitive Advantage.def: Something a company has that lets it beat competitors consistently — could be a strong brand, patents, network effects, or just being way more efficient.
Competitive Advantage.whyThisMatters: Companies with real competitive advantages (Buffett calls them 'moats') can keep earning fat profits for years — spotting durable moats is how you find long-term winners, not just flash-in-the-pan stocks.

Concentration Risk.def: When too much of your money is riding on one stock, one sector, or one geography. You're basically putting all your eggs in one basket.
Concentration Risk.whyThisMatters: Even if you're totally right about a stock, crazy stuff happens — a single position blowing up can wreck your whole portfolio, so position sizing matters as much as picking winners.

Confirmation Bias.def: You only pay attention to info that confirms what you already believe and ignore anything that contradicts it.
Confirmation Bias.whyThisMatters: This bias kills portfolios because you'll hold losers way too long and dismiss red flags — force yourself to read bear cases on stocks you love, it'll save you money.

Conglomerate.def: A giant company that owns a bunch of unrelated businesses — think a company that makes jet engines, owns a TV network, and sells insurance all under one roof.
Conglomerate.whyThisMatters: Investors usually don't love conglomerates and they trade at a discount because you can't get pure exposure to the part you actually want — which is why breaking them up often unlocks value.

Consumer Price Index (CPI).def: The government tracks how much a typical basket of stuff (groceries, gas, rent, healthcare) costs over time. When CPI goes up, that's inflation.
Consumer Price Index (CPI).whyThisMatters: CPI reports move markets hard because they tell the Fed whether to raise or cut rates — higher inflation usually means higher rates, which crushes stock valuations and bond prices.

Contingent Liability.def: A potential expense that might hit the company depending on how something uncertain plays out — like a lawsuit, warranty claim, or environmental cleanup.
Contingent Liability.whyThisMatters: These liabilities hide in the footnotes but can blow up into massive cash drains — always check disclosures for lawsuits or regulatory issues that could crater future profits.

Contrarian Investing.def: Buying when everyone's panicking and selling when everyone's euphoric. You're deliberately going against the crowd.
Contrarian Investing.whyThisMatters: The best deals show up when fear is maxed out, but being contrarian means you'll look wrong for a while — you need conviction and patience to pull it off successfully.

Convertible Bond.def: A bond that can turn into stock if the bondholder wants. You get interest payments like a normal bond, but you can swap it for shares if the stock price takes off.
Convertible Bond.whyThisMatters: Companies use these to borrow money cheaply while investors get downside protection plus upside if the stock moons — win-win if the company actually grows.

Convertible Note.def: Startups raise quick cash by issuing short-term debt that converts to equity at the next funding round, usually with a discount or cap that rewards early investors.
Convertible Note.whyThisMatters: It kicks the valuation argument down the road, but the discount and cap mean early investors get better terms than later ones — important if you're joining a seed round.

Convexity.def: How much a bond's sensitivity to interest rates changes as rates actually move. Bonds with high convexity gain more when rates drop and lose less when rates rise.
Convexity.whyThisMatters: High-convexity bonds give you asymmetric payoff — you win bigger on the upside than you lose on the downside, which is gold when rates are all over the place.

Corporate Bonds.def: Companies borrow money by selling bonds that pay you interest and return your principal at maturity. Riskier than government bonds but they pay more.
Corporate Bonds.whyThisMatters: The extra yield you get versus Treasuries reflects default risk — when that spread widens, it's the bond market screaming that corporate health is deteriorating.

Corporate Governance.def: How a company is run and who's actually calling the shots — board independence, executive pay, shareholder rights, all that structural stuff.
Corporate Governance.whyThisMatters: Bad governance is a huge red flag for fraud and value destruction — always check if insiders own meaningful stock and if the board will actually push back on the CEO.

Correlation.def: How two investments move together. Perfect correlation (+1) means they always move in sync, negative correlation (-1) means they move opposite, zero means no relationship.
Correlation.whyThisMatters: Mixing low-correlation assets smooths out your portfolio's swings without killing returns — that's why you diversify across asset classes, not just across stocks.

Cost of Capital.def: The minimum return a company needs to earn to keep its investors (both debt and equity holders) happy. It's the hurdle rate for any investment.
Cost of Capital.whyThisMatters: If a company consistently earns below its cost of capital, it's destroying value even while reporting profits — real wealth creation only happens above this line.

Cost of Debt.def: The effective interest rate a company pays to borrow, usually calculated after the tax break you get from deducting interest.
Cost of Debt.whyThisMatters: When rates rise, companies with floating-rate or short-term debt see borrowing costs spike immediately — that squeezes margins and can kill growth plans fast.

Cost of Equity.def: The return shareholders expect for investing in a company instead of putting their money somewhere else with similar risk.
