Placement Agent.whyThisMatters: Fundraising is hard—placement agents have the relationships and track record to access capital that emerging managers can't reach alone.

Platform Acquisition.def: The first company a PE firm buys in a sector as the base for adding more acquisitions later. Sets the strategy for the whole buy-and-build play.
Platform Acquisition.whyThisMatters: The platform needs strong management and infrastructure to absorb add-ons—pick wrong and the whole roll-up strategy fails.

Post-Closing Adjustment.def: The final purchase price gets adjusted after closing based on actual working capital, debt levels, or earn-out results. Can add or subtract millions from the deal value.
Post-Closing Adjustment.whyThisMatters: The target date balance sheet is just an estimate—post-closing adjustments settle up based on what was actually there at close.

Premium Analysis.def: Comparing the acquisition premium being offered to what buyers paid in similar recent deals. Used to justify whether the price is fair or generous.
Premium Analysis.whyThisMatters: If you're paying a 50% premium when comparable deals averaged 30%, you better have a good explanation for your board.

Private Placement Memorandum (PPM).def: A detailed pitch deck for private investments that lays out the company's financials, risks, and deal terms. Only accredited investors get to see it.
Private Placement Memorandum (PPM).whyThisMatters: This is what you'd read before investing in a startup or private fund — it's got all the fine print on what could go wrong.

Process Letter.def: The rulebook for an M&A auction. It tells potential buyers the timeline, how to submit bids, and what's expected at each stage.
Process Letter.whyThisMatters: This document keeps everyone on the same page during a sale process and sets clear deadlines for offers.

Proxy Fight.def: When an angry shareholder tries to kick out a company's board by convincing other shareholders to vote with them.
Proxy Fight.whyThisMatters: It's shareholder activism in action — if you own stock, your vote actually becomes a weapon in these battles.

Reinvestment Rollover.def: When sellers take some of their sale proceeds and reinvest them into the new company alongside the buyer.
Reinvestment Rollover.whyThisMatters: This keeps sellers invested in the company's future success and shows buyers they still believe in the business.

Representation and Warranty Insurance (R.def: Insurance that covers the buyer if the seller lied (or was wrong) about something important in the deal docs.
Representation and Warranty Insurance (R.whyThisMatters: It's become super common in M&A because it lets deals close faster without massive escrow holdbacks.

Reverse Break Fee.def: Money the buyer owes the seller if they back out of the deal after signing.
Reverse Break Fee.whyThisMatters: Protects sellers when buyers can't get financing or just get cold feet — especially common in PE deals.

Reverse Termination Fee.def: Same as a reverse break fee. The buyer pays if they walk away from a signed deal.
Reverse Termination Fee.whyThisMatters: This penalty discourages buyers from backing out and gives sellers compensation if the deal falls apart.

Roadshow Presentation.def: The slide deck management uses to sell investors on buying shares during an IPO or bond offering.
Roadshow Presentation.whyThisMatters: This is the pitch that determines whether investors will actually want to buy — it can make or break the deal.

Rule 144A Offering.def: A way to sell securities privately to big institutional investors without going through the full public offering process.
Rule 144A Offering.whyThisMatters: It's faster and cheaper than an IPO, which is why companies use it to raise money from qualified buyers.

Sell-Side M&A Process.def: A structured auction where a company hires bankers to find the best buyer and run a competitive bidding process.
Sell-Side M&A Process.whyThisMatters: This is how most companies get sold — competition among buyers usually means a higher price for the seller.

Senior Unsecured Debt.def: Loans that get repaid before junior debt but don't have specific collateral backing them up.
Senior Unsecured Debt.whyThisMatters: These bonds pay higher interest than secured debt because lenders take on more risk without collateral.

Share-for-Share Exchange.def: An M&A deal where the target company's shareholders get paid in the buyer's stock instead of cash.
Share-for-Share Exchange.whyThisMatters: Target shareholders become owners of the combined company, so they're betting on future growth instead of cashing out now.

Signing and Closing.def: Two big milestones in a deal: signing the contract and then actually transferring ownership (which can be weeks or months later).
Signing and Closing.whyThisMatters: The gap between these dates is when regulatory approvals happen and when deals can still fall apart.

Staple Financing.def: Pre-arranged debt financing that the seller's bankers offer to all potential buyers to make bidding easier.
Staple Financing.whyThisMatters: Speeds up the auction because buyers don't have to scramble to find their own financing before bidding.

Strategic Acquirer.def: A company buying another business to gain synergies, customers, or capabilities — not just for financial returns.
Strategic Acquirer.whyThisMatters: Strategics usually pay more than PE firms because they can actually use the business to boost their own operations.

Strategic Review.def: When a board formally evaluates all its options — selling, spinning off a division, merging, or staying independent.
Strategic Review.whyThisMatters: It's often code for 'we're probably selling' and usually leads to hiring bankers and exploring a sale.

Syndicate Desk.def: The investment banking team that coordinates who gets to buy shares in a new offering and manages the whole distribution process.
Syndicate Desk.whyThisMatters: They're the ones building the book of demand and deciding which investors get allocated shares in hot IPOs.

Syndicated Loan.def: A massive loan provided by a group of banks instead of just one, spreading the risk across multiple lenders.
Syndicated Loan.whyThisMatters: This is how most big LBO deals get financed — no single bank wants to lend billions alone.

Tag-Along Right.def: Lets minority shareholders sell their stake on the same terms if a majority owner decides to sell.
Tag-Along Right.whyThisMatters: Protects smaller investors from getting stuck holding shares when bigger players exit at a premium price.

Takeover Defense.def: Legal tricks companies use to make themselves harder or more expensive to buy without their blessing.
Takeover Defense.whyThisMatters: Things like poison pills and staggered boards can kill hostile takeovers — or at least force buyers to negotiate.

Target IRR.def: The minimum annual return (usually 20-25%) that a PE firm needs to hit to justify doing a deal.
Target IRR.whyThisMatters: If a deal can't realistically hit this number, PE firms won't bid — it's their internal hurdle rate.

Teaser Document.def: A one-page anonymous summary of a company for sale, sent to potential buyers to gauge interest before sharing real details.
Teaser Document.whyThisMatters: It's the first step in a sale process — if buyers aren't interested after the teaser, they don't get more info.

Tender Offer Premium.def: How much above the current stock price an acquirer offers to convince shareholders to sell their shares quickly.
Tender Offer Premium.whyThisMatters: A bigger premium signals urgency and often gets more shareholders to agree to sell.

Term Sheet.def: A non-binding document outlining the key financial and legal terms of a deal before lawyers draft the real contracts.
Term Sheet.whyThisMatters: This is where you negotiate the big stuff — price, structure, conditions — before spending months on legal docs.

Total Transaction Value.def: The real all-in cost of buying a company: equity price plus debt you're taking on plus fees and adjustments.
Total Transaction Value.whyThisMatters: The headline purchase price doesn't tell the whole story — you need to include debt and other costs to know what you're really paying.

Transaction Multiples.def: Valuation multiples (like EV/EBITDA) paid in recent similar deals, used to judge whether a price is fair.
Transaction Multiples.whyThisMatters: Bankers use these comps to argue whether a deal is expensive or cheap compared to what others paid recently.

Two-Stage Bidding Process.def: An auction with a first round of rough bids to narrow the field, then a second round where finalists submit binding offers.
Two-Stage Bidding Process.whyThisMatters: Saves everyone time by weeding out low bidders early before the expensive due diligence phase.

Universe of Buyers.def: The full list of every company and PE firm that might realistically want to buy a business.
Universe of Buyers.whyThisMatters: Finding and contacting the right buyers is half the job in M&A — miss someone and you might leave money on the table.

Value Creation Analysis.def: A breakdown showing how PE firms make money on a deal: through debt paydown, multiple expansion, or growing earnings.
Value Creation Analysis.whyThisMatters: Helps you see where returns actually come from in a buyout and whether the plan is realistic.

Vendor Due Diligence (VDD).def: Due diligence the seller pays for upfront and shares with all bidders to speed up the process.
Vendor Due Diligence (VDD).whyThisMatters: Reduces surprises and lets buyers move faster since they're not starting from scratch on diligence.

Voting Agreement.def: A contract where key shareholders promise to vote yes on a deal.
Voting Agreement.whyThisMatters: Locks in support before announcing the deal publicly, reducing the risk that shareholders will torpedo it later.

Working Capital Peg.def: The target level of working capital the seller agrees to deliver at closing. If it's higher or lower, the price adjusts.
Working Capital Peg.whyThisMatters: Prevents sellers from draining cash right before closing and ensures the business has enough resources to operate.

Blended IRR.def: The overall return across all investments in a PE fund, weighted by how much was invested in each deal.
Blended IRR.whyThisMatters: This is the fund-level performance metric — one home run can make up for several duds.

Buy-and-Build Strategy.def: PE firms buy a decent-sized company (the platform) and then grow it by acquiring smaller competitors or complementary businesses.
Buy-and-Build Strategy.whyThisMatters: It's one of the most common ways PE creates value — rolling up fragmented industries into one bigger, more valuable company.

Buyout Multiple.def: The EV/EBITDA multiple a PE firm pays to acquire a company.
Buyout Multiple.whyThisMatters: Lower entry multiples make it easier to generate strong returns — paying 12x vs. 8x EBITDA changes everything.

Capital Call Schedule.def: The timeline showing when and how much money LPs need to wire to the PE fund as deals get done.
Capital Call Schedule.whyThisMatters: Helps LPs plan their cash needs — you commit upfront but don't pay it all at once.

Catch-Up Provision.def: After LPs get their preferred return, the GP takes a bigger cut of profits until they hit their target carry percentage.
Catch-Up Provision.whyThisMatters: It's how GPs quickly get to their 20% share once the fund clears the hurdle rate.

Co-Investment Right.def: An LP's ability to invest extra money directly into specific deals alongside the fund, usually at lower or zero fees.
Co-Investment Right.whyThisMatters: It's a perk for big LPs that lets them boost returns on deals they really like without paying full management fees.

Committed Capital.def: The total amount an LP has legally promised to contribute to a PE fund when called.
Committed Capital.whyThisMatters: You're legally on the hook for this money even if it hasn't been called yet — defaulting has serious consequences.

Control Buyout.def: A PE deal where the firm buys a majority stake and gets full control over operations and strategy.
Control Buyout.whyThisMatters: Control lets PE firms make big changes to drive returns — it's the core of traditional buyout investing.

Deal-by-Deal Waterfall.def: A way for PE fund managers to get paid their performance cut (carry) every time they sell an investment, instead of waiting until the whole fund wraps up. Each deal gets treated separately.
Deal-by-Deal Waterfall.whyThisMatters: GPs can take home profits sooner and more consistently, but it also means they might cash out on winners while LPs are still stuck with losers.

Debt Push-Down.def: After a PE firm buys a company, they move the acquisition debt from their holding structure directly onto the company's own balance sheet. Now the company itself is carrying that loan.
Debt Push-Down.whyThisMatters: This makes the portfolio company look way more leveraged on paper and affects how much additional debt it can take on later.

Equity Contribution.def: The actual cash a PE firm (and sometimes management or co-investors) puts up to cover the equity slice of a leveraged buyout. Usually 30-40% of the total deal price.
Equity Contribution.whyThisMatters: This is the real money at risk—everything else is borrowed, so understanding this split is key to figuring out how aggressive a deal is.

Exit Planning.def: Getting a PE-backed company ready to sell, go public, or refinance. Think cleaning up financials, sharpening the story, and timing the market—usually starts a year or two before the actual exit.
Exit Planning.whyThisMatters: A clean, well-prepped exit can mean the difference between a 2x and a 3x return, so this is where deals are actually won or lost.

First-Loss Capital.def: Money that gets wiped out first if things go sideways in a structured deal. It's like being the human shield for other investors who want safer returns.
First-Loss Capital.whyThisMatters: Taking first-loss means you get paid more when things work, but you're also the first to lose everything if they don't.

Fund Economics.def: How a PE fund charges fees and splits profits. The classic setup: 2% annual management fee on committed capital, plus 20% of the profits above a certain return threshold.
Fund Economics.whyThisMatters: Understanding '2-and-20' is table stakes for anyone working in or investing with PE—it's how GPs actually make their money.

Fund Level Waterfall.def: The GP only gets their performance cut after the entire fund has returned all investor money plus a minimum return. Everything's pooled together instead of calculated deal-by-deal.
Fund Level Waterfall.whyThisMatters: Way more LP-friendly than deal-by-deal because the GP can't cherry-pick profits from winners while losses are still sitting in the fund.

Growth Equity.def: Investing in profitable, fast-growing companies that don't need a total overhaul—just capital to scale. You're buying a minority stake, not taking over.
Growth Equity.whyThisMatters: It's the sweet spot between venture capital's chaos and traditional buyouts' heavy lifting—lower risk, but also less control.

Holding Period Extension.def: When a PE fund keeps an investment way longer than originally planned, either through special vehicles or by extending the fund's life. Translation: they can't find a good exit.
Holding Period Extension.whyThisMatters: Usually means the market isn't cooperating or the company isn't hitting targets—either way, it's tying up capital and testing LP patience.

In-Kind Distribution.def: Instead of selling a portfolio company and giving LPs cash, the PE fund just hands them the actual shares. You become a direct shareholder.
In-Kind Distribution.whyThisMatters: Common after IPOs when the fund wants to let LPs decide when to sell—gives you more control but also means you're now managing individual stock positions.

Investment Committee Memo.def: The big internal pitch deck that deal teams write to convince their own firm's investment committee to greenlight a deal. All your analysis ends up here.
Investment Committee Memo.whyThisMatters: This is the real final product of weeks of modeling and due diligence—if the IC memo doesn't sell the deal, nothing else matters.

Investment Period.def: The window—usually 3 to 5 years—when a PE fund can make brand new investments. After that, they can only add money to companies they already own.
Investment Period.whyThisMatters: Once the investment period closes, the fund shifts from hunting deals to managing and exiting what they've already bought.

IRR Sensitivity.def: Running the numbers to see how your projected return changes if you tweak assumptions like exit multiple, EBITDA growth, or how long you hold the investment.
IRR Sensitivity.whyThisMatters: Shows you which assumptions actually move the needle on returns and where your deal is most vulnerable to reality not matching the plan.

LP Advisory Committee (LPAC).def: A group of major limited partners who advise the GP and sign off on potential conflicts of interest. Think of it as the board representing investor interests.
LP Advisory Committee (LPAC).whyThisMatters: It's a key governance check to make sure GPs aren't doing shady stuff that benefits them at LP expense.

Management Fee Offset.def: When the PE firm earns advisory or transaction fees from portfolio companies, those fees get subtracted from the management fee LPs pay. Basically a rebate.
Management Fee Offset.whyThisMatters: More LP-friendly funds do 100% offsets, meaning they're not double-dipping on fees from both the fund and the companies.

Management Incentive Plan (MIP).def: An equity pool carved out for the portfolio company's management team, with payouts tied to hitting return targets when the PE firm eventually sells.
Management Incentive Plan (MIP).whyThisMatters: Aligns the CEO and leadership with PE owners so everyone's focused on the same exit payday—critical for execution.

Management Ratchet.def: Popular in European PE deals—management's equity stake automatically grows if the exit return crosses certain thresholds. Hit higher returns, get more equity.
Management Ratchet.whyThisMatters: Creates massive upside for executives if they crush it, which keeps them hyper-motivated to deliver for investors.

Management Rollover.def: When a company's existing executives sell to a PE firm but reinvest some of their sale proceeds into the new deal as equity owners. They're betting on themselves twice.
Management Rollover.whyThisMatters: Huge positive signal—if the people who know the business best are willing to stay invested, that tells you they believe in what's coming.

Minority Buyout.def: A PE firm buys less than 50% of a company, so the original owners stay in control. Lower risk, smaller check, but also less say in how things run.
Minority Buyout.whyThisMatters: You get access to founder-led businesses that won't sell majority stakes, but you're also betting the founder keeps performing without you calling the shots.

Operational Alpha.def: Returns generated by actually improving how the business operates—growing revenue, cutting costs, fixing processes—not just from leverage or riding a hot market.
Operational Alpha.whyThisMatters: This is what separates real value creators from financial engineers who just got lucky with timing and cheap debt.

Operational Due Diligence.def: Deep dive into how a target company actually runs—supply chain, tech stack, management quality, customer relationships. Goes way beyond the financials.
Operational Due Diligence.whyThisMatters: Numbers can lie or hide problems, so this is where you figure out if the business can actually deliver on the growth story you're underwriting.

Operational Value Creation.def: The playbook PE firms use to boost EBITDA after buying a company: grow revenue, expand margins, and squeeze more cash out of working capital.
Operational Value Creation.whyThisMatters: This is how PE firms justify their fees—if they can't operationally improve a business, they're just leveraged stock pickers.

Partial Exit.def: Selling some but not all of your stake in a portfolio company. You take chips off the table while keeping skin in the game for future upside.
Partial Exit.whyThisMatters: Lets you return cash to LPs and lock in gains while still participating if the company keeps growing—risk management plus optionality.

PIK Interest.def: Instead of paying interest in cash, you pay it by adding to the loan balance. The debt keeps growing, but you preserve cash flow in the short term.
PIK Interest.whyThisMatters: Helpful when a company's tight on cash, but you're basically letting leverage snowball—eventually that bill comes due with interest.

Portfolio Company.def: Any business a PE fund owns a stake in. These are the actual companies your fund invested in and is trying to grow and sell.
Portfolio Company.whyThisMatters: As an analyst or associate, these companies are your job—you'll be tracking performance, supporting management, and prepping them for exit.

Post-Acquisition Integration.def: The messy work of actually combining an acquisition with your existing platform—merging systems, aligning teams, consolidating processes. Harder than it sounds.
Post-Acquisition Integration.whyThisMatters: Most buy-and-build strategies fail here, not in deal sourcing—if you can't integrate well, you've just bought yourself expensive chaos.

Secondaries Market.def: Where investors buy and sell existing stakes in PE funds or portfolio companies from other investors, instead of investing fresh capital into new funds.
Secondaries Market.whyThisMatters: Gives LPs a way to get liquid before funds fully exit and creates opportunities for buyers to enter established funds at a discount.

Secondary Buyout (SBO).def: One PE firm sells a portfolio company to another PE firm. It's like passing the baton—PE to PE instead of PE to strategic buyer or public markets.
Secondary Buyout (SBO).whyThisMatters: This is now the most common exit path in PE, which tells you the industry's gotten huge and sometimes just recycles companies among itself.

Subscription Line of Credit.def: Short-term borrowing a PE fund uses to fund deals before actually calling money from LPs. Keeps capital calls smooth but can juice IRR numbers artificially.
Subscription Line of Credit.whyThisMatters: Makes reported IRRs look better because the fund isn't 'using' LP capital for as long, but it's mostly a timing trick that flatters performance.

Total Value to Paid-In (TVPI).def: Total value you've gotten back (cash distributions plus what's still invested) divided by total capital you put in. Basically your multiple on invested capital, realized and unrealized.
Total Value to Paid-In (TVPI).whyThisMatters: Unlike IRR, TVPI can't be gamed with timing tricks—it's a clean snapshot of whether a fund has actually created value or not.

Transaction Sponsor.def: The PE firm running the show on a buyout. They structure the deal, line up financing, negotiate terms, and drive the whole process.
Transaction Sponsor.whyThisMatters: Being the sponsor means you control strategy and economics—you're the quarterback, not just a co-investor along for the ride.

Unitranche Debt.def: A single loan that blends first-lien and second-lien debt into one facility with one interest rate. Simpler than juggling multiple lenders with different terms.
Unitranche Debt.whyThisMatters: Popular in middle-market deals because it's faster to arrange and easier to manage, even if the blended rate is a bit higher.

Unrealized Value.def: What your PE fund's unsold investments are currently worth on paper. It's part of your total value, but you haven't actually cashed out yet.
Unrealized Value.whyThisMatters: High unrealized value looks good in reports, but remember it's just an estimate—real returns only happen when you actually exit and distribute cash.

Unlevered IRR.def: Your return calculated as if you bought the company with zero debt—just pure equity returns. Strips out the leverage boost to show operating performance.
Unlevered IRR.whyThisMatters: Helps you see if value came from actually improving the business or just from stacking on debt and riding financial engineering.

Vintage Year Performance.def: Comparing how PE funds raised in the same year performed against each other. Tells you whether market conditions when you invested mattered more than skill.
Vintage Year Performance.whyThisMatters: Some vintages just got luckier with market timing—understanding this helps separate skill from being in the right place at the right time.

Waterfall Distribution.def: The specific order cash from exits flows to LPs and the GP. Covers who gets paid first, when carry kicks in, and how profits get split.
Waterfall Distribution.whyThisMatters: This structure determines when and how much GPs actually earn on performance—it's the core economic deal between LPs and the fund manager.

Write-Up.def: When a PE fund increases the book value of an investment because the company's performing better or market conditions improved. Opposite of a write-down.
Write-Up.whyThisMatters: Write-ups boost your fund's reported NAV and signal things are going well, but remember it's unrealized until you actually sell.

Accelerator Program.def: A short, intense program (think Y Combinator or Techstars) where early startups get mentorship, resources, and a small check in exchange for equity. Usually lasts 3-4 months.
Accelerator Program.whyThisMatters: Great for super early founders who need structure and connections fast—the network and brand often matter more than the actual funding.

Anti-Dilution Ratchet.def: A protection clause in VC deals that adjusts an investor's share price downward if the company raises money at a lower valuation later. Shields VCs from down rounds.
Anti-Dilution Ratchet.whyThisMatters: Protects early investors from getting crushed in down rounds, but full ratchets can absolutely wreck founder ownership if things go south.

Bridge Round.def: Short-term funding to keep a startup alive until their next big fundraise. Could signal strong momentum and strategic timing, or desperation and trouble raising.
Bridge Round.whyThisMatters: Context matters here—a bridge from top investors buying time before a big Series B is very different from a messy bridge to avoid running out of cash.

Broad-Based Weighted Average.def: When a startup raises money at a lower price than before, this anti-dilution formula protects early investors by adjusting their share price — but it factors in all shares (including options), so it's way less harsh on founders than full ratchet.
Broad-Based Weighted Average.whyThisMatters: It's the founder-friendly anti-dilution method; early investors get some protection in a down round without totally crushing founder equity.

Burn Multiple.def: How many dollars a company burns through to add one dollar of annual recurring revenue. If you burn $3M to add $1M in ARR, your burn multiple is 3x.
Burn Multiple.whyThisMatters: Shows how efficiently a startup converts cash into growth — investors love burn multiples under 2x.

Cliff Period.def: You don't get any equity until you've stuck around for this initial period (usually a year). Leave before the cliff hits? You walk away with zero shares.
Cliff Period.whyThisMatters: If you join a startup and quit at month 11, you get nothing — the cliff protects companies from people who bounce early.

Convertible Equity.def: Like a SAFE or other instrument that turns into actual equity later when the company raises a priced round, but it doesn't accrue interest or have a maturity date like convertible debt does.
Convertible Equity.whyThisMatters: Cleaner than a convertible note because it's not technically debt — no interest piling up or awkward loan dynamics with founders.

Cram-Down Round.def: When a struggling startup raises money at a valuation way lower than before, massively diluting everyone who invested earlier. It's basically a reset button that hurts.
Cram-Down Round.whyThisMatters: Usually means the company's in trouble and early investors are about to see their ownership shrink dramatically.

Customer Discovery.def: Talking to potential customers before you build anything to make sure the problem you think exists actually does. It's the opposite of 'build it and they will come.'
Customer Discovery.whyThisMatters: Prevents you from wasting months building something nobody wants — validate the problem first, product second.

Deal Flow.def: The stream of startups pitching to a VC firm. Good deal flow means seeing high-quality companies early; bad deal flow means looking at leftovers.
Deal Flow.whyThisMatters: A VC's biggest competitive edge isn't capital, it's seeing the best deals before everyone else does.

Dilutive Financing.def: Any time a company issues new shares to raise money, everyone's ownership percentage goes down. That's dilution.
Dilutive Financing.whyThisMatters: Every funding round shrinks your slice of the pie — founders need to balance raising capital with keeping enough equity to stay motivated.

Distributed to Paid-In (DPI).def: Actual cash returned to investors divided by the cash they put in. A DPI of 2.0x means you've doubled their money — and they've actually received it.
Distributed to Paid-In (DPI).whyThisMatters: The only VC metric that shows real, banked returns rather than paper gains — you can't spend unrealized value.

Drag-Along Provision.def: If a majority of shareholders want to sell the company, they can force everyone else to sell too. Prevents one stubborn person from blocking a deal.
Drag-Along Provision.whyThisMatters: Protects VCs when they want to exit but a founder or minor shareholder refuses to sell — majority rules.

Dual Trigger Acceleration.def: Your unvested equity speeds up only if two things happen: the company gets acquired AND you lose your job. One without the other doesn't count.
Dual Trigger Acceleration.whyThisMatters: Protects employees in acquisitions without giving them a reason to push for a sale just to cash out early.

Early-Stage Venture.def: Investing in companies at Seed or Series A before they've really figured out how to make money consistently. High risk, huge upside.
Early-Stage Venture.whyThisMatters: Where you can turn $100K into $50M — but most investments fail, so you need a portfolio approach.

Employee Stock Option Pool.def: A chunk of equity the company sets aside specifically to grant as options to employees. VCs usually make you create this before they invest.
Employee Stock Option Pool.whyThisMatters: Comes out of founder ownership, not investor ownership — so negotiate the pool size before the round closes.

First Check.def: The very first institutional money into a startup, usually at pre-seed or seed stage when it's just an idea and a pitch deck.
First Check.whyThisMatters: First-check investors take massive risk for the cheapest entry price — if the company works, they'll have the best returns.

Follow-On Investment.def: When a VC that already invested in your company writes another check in your next round. It's a vote of confidence.
Follow-On Investment.whyThisMatters: Signals strong conviction in the company and helps the VC maintain their ownership percentage as you grow.

Founder Dilution.def: Every time you raise money, your ownership percentage shrinks. Raise three rounds and you might go from owning 100% to owning 15%.
Founder Dilution.whyThisMatters: Understanding dilution helps you plan how much to raise and when — you want enough equity left at exit to make it worthwhile.

Founder-Market Fit.def: Does your background actually make you the right person to solve this problem? VCs care about this almost as much as the idea itself.
Founder-Market Fit.whyThisMatters: A first-time founder building enterprise AI is less compelling than an ex-Google AI researcher doing the same thing.

Full-Ratchet Anti-Dilution.def: The nuclear option of investor protection. If the company raises at a lower price later, early investors get to retroactively convert their shares at that new, cheaper price.
Full-Ratchet Anti-Dilution.whyThisMatters: Absolutely brutal for founders — avoid this term at all costs because it can wipe out your equity in a down round.

Growth Round.def: Big financing rounds (Series C and later) for companies that already have product-market fit and just need cash to scale faster.
Growth Round.whyThisMatters: Less risky than early rounds because the business model works — you're funding execution, not experimentation.

Incubator Program.def: Organizations that give early startups office space, mentorship, and sometimes a small check in exchange for a bit of equity (usually 5-7%).
Incubator Program.whyThisMatters: Can jumpstart your network and learning curve, but make sure the equity trade-off is worth what you're getting.

Information Rights.def: VCs negotiate the right to see your financials and company updates regularly — usually monthly or quarterly reporting.
Information Rights.whyThisMatters: Standard in any VC deal; they're writing big checks and want to track how you're spending their money.

Key Man Clause.def: If the star partners at a VC firm leave, this clause pauses new investments or lets investors pull their money out of the fund.
Key Man Clause.whyThisMatters: Protects people who invested in a fund because of specific partners — if those partners bail, so can the LPs.

Late-Stage Venture.def: Funding companies at Series C and beyond that have proven their model and are scaling toward IPO or acquisition.
Late-Stage Venture.whyThisMatters: Lower risk than early-stage, but you're also not getting in at a $10M valuation — expect smaller multiples on exit.

Lead Investor.def: The VC running the show in a funding round — they set the terms, do most of the diligence, write the biggest check, and usually take a board seat.
Lead Investor.whyThisMatters: Your lead investor matters way more than other investors because they're setting your valuation and terms.

Milestone Financing.def: Instead of handing you all the money upfront, investors release it in chunks as you hit specific goals like launching product or hitting revenue targets.
Milestone Financing.whyThisMatters: Reduces investor risk but adds pressure on founders to hit targets or risk running out of cash.

Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR).def: The predictable monthly revenue from subscriptions. If you have 100 customers paying $50/month, your MRR is $5,000.
Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR).whyThisMatters: The heartbeat metric for SaaS companies — investors obsess over MRR growth because it's recurring and predictable.

Multi-Stage Participation.def: When the same investor keeps writing checks in your Series A, B, and C rounds instead of getting diluted out.
Multi-Stage Participation.whyThisMatters: Shows serious conviction in your company and helps your best investors maintain meaningful ownership as you grow.

Narrow-Based Weighted Average.def: An anti-dilution formula that only counts preferred shares when recalculating prices after a down round — way harsher on founders than broad-based.
Narrow-Based Weighted Average.whyThisMatters: More investor-friendly than broad-based weighted average; gives investors stronger protection but dilutes founders more.

Net Dollar Retention.def: Start with $100K in revenue from existing customers. A year later, after some churn and some upsells, what's that cohort worth? If it's $120K, your NDR is 120%.
Net Dollar Retention.whyThisMatters: NDR above 100% means your existing customers are spending more over time — a sign of a sticky, valuable product.

Network Externality.def: Your product gets way more valuable as more people use it. Think Facebook, Venmo, or Uber — they're useless if you're the only user.
Network Externality.whyThisMatters: Creates a defensive moat that's nearly impossible to break because every new user makes it harder to switch to a competitor.

Non-Participating Preferred.def: At exit, investors pick one: get their liquidation preference back OR convert to common and share in the upside. They can't do both.
Non-Participating Preferred.whyThisMatters: Better for founders than participating preferred — limits how much investors can take in an exit.

Participating Preferred.def: Investors get their money back first (liquidation preference), THEN convert to common and take another slice of what's left. Double-dipping.
Participating Preferred.whyThisMatters: Really investor-friendly and rough on founders — in modest exits, investors can take a disproportionate share of proceeds.

Pay-to-Play Provision.def: Existing investors have to invest in the next round or they lose special rights like anti-dilution protection. Basically: put up or shut up.
Pay-to-Play Provision.whyThisMatters: Punishes investors who won't support the company in tough times — keeps everyone aligned and committed.

Portfolio Construction.def: How a VC decides to spread their fund across different stages, sectors, and check sizes to balance risk and maximize returns.
Portfolio Construction.whyThisMatters: Shapes whether a fund swings for massive home runs or plays it safer with later-stage bets — affects what kinds of companies they target.

Power Law Distribution.def: In venture, one or two huge wins generate almost all the returns while most investments fail or return little. It's not a normal distribution — it's wildly skewed.
Power Law Distribution.whyThisMatters: Explains why VCs pass on 'pretty good' companies and only invest in ideas with 100x potential — they need massive outliers to make the fund work.

Pre-Seed Round.def: The earliest real funding round, usually $500K to $2M from angels or micro-VCs when you're still validating the idea and maybe have a prototype.
Pre-Seed Round.whyThisMatters: Gets you enough runway to build an MVP and get initial traction before raising a proper Seed round.

Pro Rata Right.def: Investors get the option to put more money in future rounds to keep their ownership percentage from shrinking. It's a 'right,' not an obligation.
Pro Rata Right.whyThisMatters: Lets your best early investors maintain their stake as you grow — super valuable in hot companies where later rounds are oversubscribed.

Product-Market Fit.def: When you've built something people desperately want. You'll know you have it when customers are pulling the product from you, not when you're pushing it on them.
Product-Market Fit.whyThisMatters: The single most important milestone for a startup — without it, growth is expensive and unsustainable.

Protective Provisions.def: VCs negotiate veto power over big decisions like selling the company, raising debt, or issuing new shares. Think of it as investor insurance.
Protective Provisions.whyThisMatters: Prevents founders from making major moves without investor approval — standard in any institutional deal.

Qualified IPO.def: An IPO that hits certain minimum size and price thresholds written into your charter. When it happens, all preferred stock auto-converts to common.
Qualified IPO.whyThisMatters: Cleans up your cap table for public markets — investors give up their special rights once the company goes public successfully.

Redemption Right.def: A VC investor's ability to force the startup to buy back their shares at the original price (plus any owed dividends) after a certain time period passes.
Redemption Right.whyThisMatters: If things go south, preferred investors can demand their cash back — which can drain a startup's bank account fast.

Residual Value to Paid-In (RVPI).def: Shows what a VC fund's remaining investments are currently worth compared to how much money investors originally put in.
Residual Value to Paid-In (RVPI).whyThisMatters: Tells you the unrealized potential sitting in a fund's portfolio — what hasn't been sold yet but could turn into actual returns.

Right of First Refusal (ROFR).def: Before you can sell your shares to someone new, existing investors or the company get first dibs to match that offer and buy them instead.
Right of First Refusal (ROFR).whyThisMatters: VCs use this to block founders from selling to outsiders — keeps control in familiar hands.

Safe Note (SAFE).def: A simple agreement where you give a startup money now, and it converts into actual shares later when they raise a real funding round — no interest, no repayment date.
Safe Note (SAFE).whyThisMatters: Way simpler than convertible notes since there's no debt or interest to track — just future equity when the timing's right.

Series A Round.def: The first major fundraising round where a startup gets serious institutional money, usually between $2M and $15M.
Series A Round.whyThisMatters: Proves your business model actually works with real customers paying real money — a huge credibility milestone.

Series B Round.def: After proving the model works, this round brings in cash to scale up your team, sales, and marketing machine.
Series B Round.whyThisMatters: You've found product-market fit — now it's time to pour gas on the fire and grow fast.

Series C Round.def: Late-stage funding for companies already scaling successfully, often used to speed up growth or prep for going public.
Series C Round.whyThisMatters: At this point you're valued in the hundreds of millions and either heading toward IPO or acquisition.

Single-Trigger Acceleration.def: Your unvested equity immediately vests just because the company got acquired, regardless of whether you stay or leave.
Single-Trigger Acceleration.whyThisMatters: Great for founders, but buyers often hate it since you can cash out and bail right after the deal closes.

Staged Investment.def: Instead of handing over all the money upfront, investors release it in chunks as you hit specific milestones.
Staged Investment.whyThisMatters: Lowers risk for VCs and keeps you accountable — you only get more cash when you prove you can execute.

Super Pro Rata Right.def: Lets an investor put in more money than their ownership percentage would normally allow in the next funding round.
Super Pro Rata Right.whyThisMatters: Top investors negotiate this in hot deals so they can double down if the company takes off.

Term Sheet (VC).def: A non-binding outline of what the investment deal will look like — covers valuation, who gets what in a sale, and board control.
Term Sheet (VC).whyThisMatters: It's not legally binding yet, but it sets the framework for everything that matters in your funding deal.

Total Addressable Market Sizing.def: Figuring out how much revenue your startup could potentially make if you somehow captured every single customer in your market.
Total Addressable Market Sizing.whyThisMatters: Every investor pitch starts here — if the market's too small, the upside isn't worth their time.

Tranche Financing.def: Money gets released in stages tied to hitting certain performance goals or time milestones.
Tranche Financing.whyThisMatters: Keeps both sides honest — investors lower their risk, and you get rewarded for executing.

Unicorn Company.def: A private startup worth over $1 billion.
Unicorn Company.whyThisMatters: Used to be rare and magical, now it's the benchmark every ambitious founder is chasing.

Up Round.def: Raising money at a higher valuation than your last round.
Up Round.whyThisMatters: Signal that you're growing fast and investors are fighting to get in — the opposite of a down round.

Valuation Cap.def: The ceiling on how high the company's valuation can be when your convertible note or SAFE turns into actual equity.
Valuation Cap.whyThisMatters: Protects early investors if the next round's valuation skyrockets — they still convert at the capped price.

Vesting Schedule.def: The timeline over which you actually earn full ownership of the equity you've been granted.
Vesting Schedule.whyThisMatters: Standard is four years with a one-year cliff — stick around or you lose unvested shares.

Adjusted Enterprise Value.def: Enterprise value tweaked to account for weird stuff like excess cash, off-balance-sheet debts, and non-operating assets so you can compare companies cleanly.
Adjusted Enterprise Value.whyThisMatters: Makes apples-to-apples comparisons actually possible when companies have messy balance sheets.

Asset-Based Valuation.def: Valuing a company by adding up what all its assets are worth and subtracting what it owes.
Asset-Based Valuation.whyThisMatters: Works best for holding companies, real estate firms, or distressed businesses where assets matter more than earnings.

Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM).def: A formula that calculates what return investors should expect from a stock based on how risky it is compared to the overall market.
Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM).whyThisMatters: The go-to model for figuring out cost of equity — used everywhere in finance.

Cost Approach.def: Valuing something based on what it would cost to replace or rebuild it from scratch today.
Cost Approach.whyThisMatters: Common for insurance claims and real assets like buildings or equipment where replacement cost makes sense.

Cyclically Adjusted P/E Ratio (CAPE).def: A P/E ratio that uses the average inflation-adjusted earnings over the past 10 years to smooth out boom-and-bust cycles.
Cyclically Adjusted P/E Ratio (CAPE).whyThisMatters: Helps you see if the market's overvalued without getting fooled by one great (or terrible) year.

Discounted Dividend Model.def: Values a stock by estimating all the dividends it'll pay in the future and discounting them back to today's dollars.
Discounted Dividend Model.whyThisMatters: Best for stable, dividend-paying companies — doesn't work well for growth stocks that reinvest everything.

EV to Gross Profit.def: Enterprise value divided by gross profit — used when a high-growth company isn't profitable yet.
EV to Gross Profit.whyThisMatters: SaaS and tech startups often get valued this way since EBITDA is still negative while they scale.

EV/EBIT Multiple.def: Enterprise value divided by operating profit (before interest and taxes) — treats depreciation as a real cost unlike EBITDA.
EV/EBIT Multiple.whyThisMatters: Better than EV/EBITDA for capital-intensive businesses where depreciation actually matters.

Excess Return on Invested Capital.def: How much more return a company earns on its investments compared to what it costs them to raise that money (ROIC minus WACC).
Excess Return on Invested Capital.whyThisMatters: Positive excess returns mean the company creates real value — negative means it's destroying shareholder wealth.

Floor Valuation.def: The absolute minimum a company should be worth based on a specific method like what a private equity buyer could pay or its asset value.
Floor Valuation.whyThisMatters: Useful in negotiations — sets the baseline price below which a deal doesn't make sense.

Forward Revenue Multiple.def: Enterprise value divided by what the company expects to make in revenue over the next twelve months.
Forward Revenue Multiple.whyThisMatters: Standard for valuing fast-growing companies that aren't profitable yet but have strong revenue momentum.

Fundamental Value.def: What an asset is actually worth based on the cash it'll generate over time, ignoring what the market's currently paying for it.
Fundamental Value.whyThisMatters: The foundation of value investing — buy when market price is below fundamental value.

Growth-Value Trade-Off.def: The constant decision between reinvesting profits to grow faster versus returning cash to shareholders — resolved by comparing reinvestment returns to cost of capital.
Growth-Value Trade-Off.whyThisMatters: Core tension in corporate strategy — grow or pay dividends, and it depends on whether you can beat your cost of capital.

Income Multiple.def: Any valuation multiple based on earnings — could be EBITDA, EBIT, or net income.
Income Multiple.whyThisMatters: The standard language everyone uses to talk about what a business is worth.

Intrinsic Growth Rate.def: The fastest a company can grow using only its own cash flow without raising outside money — calculated as ROIC times reinvestment rate.
Intrinsic Growth Rate.whyThisMatters: Shows sustainable growth potential without diluting shareholders or taking on debt.

LBO Floor Valuation.def: The max price a private equity firm can pay and still hit their minimum return target.
LBO Floor Valuation.whyThisMatters: Sets the floor in M&A auctions — no PE buyer will go lower than this.

Market-Implied Growth Rate.def: The growth rate baked into a stock's current price — basically what has to happen for the stock to be fairly valued right now.
Market-Implied Growth Rate.whyThisMatters: Reverse-engineers the market's expectations so you can decide if they're realistic or insane.

Mark-to-Model.def: Valuing something using a financial model instead of actual market prices because there's no active market for it.
Mark-to-Model.whyThisMatters: Common for illiquid stuff like private equity holdings — leaves room for optimistic (or sketchy) estimates.

Mid-Cycle Earnings.def: Earnings adjusted to what they'd look like at a normal point in the economic cycle, not at a peak or trough.
Mid-Cycle Earnings.whyThisMatters: Removes boom-and-bust distortions so you can value cyclical businesses more accurately.

Normalized EBITDA.def: EBITDA cleaned up by removing one-time expenses, unusual items, and other junk to show true recurring profitability.
Normalized EBITDA.whyThisMatters: Shows the real earning power of a business without all the noise.

Normalized Earnings Power.def: What a business can sustainably earn in a typical year once you strip out accounting games and one-off events.
Normalized Earnings Power.whyThisMatters: The baseline for valuing any company through a full business cycle.

Parity Value.def: For convertible bonds, what the bond would be worth if you converted it into stock right now at the current stock price.
Parity Value.whyThisMatters: Helps you figure out if a convertible bond is trading cheap or expensive compared to the underlying stock.

Peer Group Multiple.def: The median or average valuation multiple of similar publicly traded companies used as a benchmark.
Peer Group Multiple.whyThisMatters: The starting point for relative valuation — if peers trade at 10x EBITDA, that's your baseline.

Price to Operating Cash Flow.def: You take a company's market cap and divide it by the cash it actually generates from operations. It's tougher to game than P/E because cash is harder to fake than earnings.
Price to Operating Cash Flow.whyThisMatters: It's a cleaner valuation metric when you don't trust reported earnings—cash doesn't lie.

Replacement Value.def: What it'd cost to rebuild everything a company owns—factories, equipment, inventory—from scratch at today's prices.
Replacement Value.whyThisMatters: In heavy industries like manufacturing or utilities, it sets a floor for what the company should be worth.

Residual Income Model (RIM).def: Values a company by starting with its book value, then adding the present value of all future profits above what investors expect to earn. If you beat your cost of equity, that excess gets capitalized into value.
Residual Income Model (RIM).whyThisMatters: It rewards companies that generate returns above their cost of capital, not just those with big earnings.

Risk-Adjusted Discount Rate.def: A higher discount rate you use when valuing risky investments. The riskier the cash flows, the higher the rate, which pushes down the value today.
Risk-Adjusted Discount Rate.whyThisMatters: High risk means you demand a higher return—so you discount future cash harder, leading to lower valuations.

Sensitivity Table.def: A grid in your model showing how valuation changes when you tweak two key assumptions at once—like revenue growth and discount rate.
Sensitivity Table.whyThisMatters: It's the standard output in every pitch deck or model, showing how sensitive your answer is to your guesses.

Standalone Value.def: What a company's worth on its own, ignoring any benefits from merging with someone else.
Standalone Value.whyThisMatters: It's your baseline before you start adding premiums for synergies or strategic value in M&A.

Synergy Valuation.def: The extra value created when two companies merge—cost cuts, revenue boosts—discounted back to today. It's why buyers pay premiums.
Synergy Valuation.whyThisMatters: Synergies justify paying more than standalone value; without them, you're overpaying.

Through-the-Cycle Valuation.def: Valuing a company using average, mid-cycle numbers instead of whatever inflated peak or depressed trough you're in right now.
Through-the-Cycle Valuation.whyThisMatters: It smooths out the noise and gives you a more defensible, stable valuation over time.

Tobin's Q.def: Market value divided by the cost to replace all the company's assets. A Q above 1 means the market thinks those assets are worth more than their price tag.
Tobin's Q.whyThisMatters: If Q is way above 1, the market's pricing in intangibles like brand or moat; below 1 might signal undervaluation.

Value Bridge.def: A chart or calculation breaking down exactly what drove the change in a company's value between two dates—growth, margins, multiples, whatever.
Value Bridge.whyThisMatters: It tells you whether value was created or destroyed, and which levers mattered most.

Valuation Discount.def: A haircut you apply to value for things like illiquidity, being a minority shareholder, or key-person risk.
Valuation Discount.whyThisMatters: Real-world stakes come with friction—discounts reflect that and keep valuations honest.

Valuation Floor.def: The lowest reasonable price you'd put on a company, based on worst-case scenarios.
Valuation Floor.whyThisMatters: It's your downside protection in negotiations and helps you avoid overpaying in tough situations.

Valuation Methodology.def: Picking the right approach—DCF, comps, precedent deals—to figure out what a company's worth. Each has pros and cons depending on the situation.
Valuation Methodology.whyThisMatters: Different methods work better in different contexts; knowing which to use makes your valuation credible.

Valuation Premium.def: A higher multiple slapped on a company because it's rare, higher quality, or strategically valuable.
Valuation Premium.whyThisMatters: Market leaders and scarce assets command premiums—understanding why helps you avoid paying too much.

Accounts Receivable Aging.def: A report splitting unpaid invoices into buckets—30 days overdue, 60 days, 90+ days—so you can see who's slow-paying and how bad it's getting.
Accounts Receivable Aging.whyThisMatters: If receivables are piling up in the old buckets, you've got collection problems or customers in trouble.

Asset Intensity.def: How many dollars of assets you need to generate a dollar of revenue. High asset intensity means you're stuck reinvesting heavily, which crushes free cash flow.
Asset Intensity.whyThisMatters: Asset-heavy businesses like airlines or manufacturing struggle to generate cash and earn strong returns.

Asset Write-Down.def: When a company admits an asset is worth less than it's carrying on the books and takes a charge to reduce its value.
Asset Write-Down.whyThisMatters: Write-downs often signal overpaid acquisitions or deteriorating business conditions—red flags for investors.

Cash Burn Analysis.def: Tracking how fast a company's spending its cash reserves each month or quarter.
Cash Burn Analysis.whyThisMatters: For startups and unprofitable companies, cash burn tells you how long they can survive before needing more funding.

Cash Flow Bridge.def: A reconciliation showing how you get from net income to operating cash flow by adding back non-cash expenses and adjusting for working capital changes.
Cash Flow Bridge.whyThisMatters: It explains why profit doesn't equal cash—critical for understanding a company's true liquidity.

Channel Inventory.def: Products sitting at distributors or retailers that the manufacturer has already counted as sales, but consumers haven't bought yet.
Channel Inventory.whyThisMatters: Companies can stuff the channel to inflate revenue short-term, but it's unsustainable and often leads to returns.

Combined Ratio.def: In insurance, it's claims paid plus operating expenses divided by premiums collected. Below 100% means they're making money on underwriting.
Combined Ratio.whyThisMatters: If it's above 100%, the insurer loses money on policies and only profits if investment returns make up the gap.

Common Size Analysis.def: You restate every line on a financial statement as a percentage of revenue (or total assets for the balance sheet) so you can compare companies of different sizes.
Common Size Analysis.whyThisMatters: It strips out size differences and lets you compare margin structure and efficiency across competitors cleanly.

Comprehensive Income.def: Net income plus other stuff that bypasses the income statement—like foreign currency translation adjustments and unrealized gains on investments.
Comprehensive Income.whyThisMatters: It gives a fuller picture of how shareholder equity changed beyond just reported net income.

Consolidated Financial Statements.def: When a parent company owns subsidiaries, it combines all their financials into one set of statements as if they're a single entity.
Consolidated Financial Statements.whyThisMatters: It's the standard for any public company with controlled subsidiaries—you see the whole group, not just the parent.

Construction in Progress.def: A balance sheet line for assets being built that aren't finished yet, so they're not generating revenue or being depreciated.
Construction in Progress.whyThisMatters: It temporarily understates depreciation and can signal big future capacity coming online.

Contra Account.def: An account that offsets another one—like accumulated depreciation reducing gross PP&E on the balance sheet.
Contra Account.whyThisMatters: Understanding contra accounts helps you read balance sheets accurately and see the real net value of assets.

Days Working Capital.def: How many days' worth of revenue is tied up in working capital (receivables plus inventory minus payables).
Days Working Capital.whyThisMatters: Lower is better—it means you're turning working capital faster and freeing up cash for other uses.

Debt Amortization Schedule.def: A timeline showing when and how much principal you have to pay back on debt.
Debt Amortization Schedule.whyThisMatters: You need this to build accurate cash flow models and LBOs—it drives your required debt service.

Deferred Compensation.def: Pay that's earned now but paid later, creating a liability on the balance sheet until it's actually handed over.
Deferred Compensation.whyThisMatters: It affects cash flow timing and can create big future cash drains when payments come due.

Dilutive Effect.def: When options, warrants, or convertible debt get exercised, they create new shares and lower everyone's earnings per share.
Dilutive Effect.whyThisMatters: Ignoring dilution overstates EPS and misvalues companies with lots of employee stock options.

EBITDA to Free Cash Flow Bridge.def: A breakdown showing all the adjustments—CapEx, working capital, taxes, interest—that explain why EBITDA and free cash flow are different numbers.
EBITDA to Free Cash Flow Bridge.whyThisMatters: EBITDA isn't cash; this bridge shows you what really matters for valuation and debt capacity.

Equity Method Investment.def: When you own 20-50% of another company and have significant influence, you recognize your share of their earnings on your income statement.
Equity Method Investment.whyThisMatters: It's common in joint ventures and strategic stakes—your P&L includes their profits even though you don't control them.

Fixed Asset Schedule.def: A roll-forward showing beginning PP&E, what you bought or sold, depreciation taken, and ending PP&E.
Fixed Asset Schedule.whyThisMatters: It's a core schedule in every three-statement model and drives CapEx and depreciation.

Free Cash Flow Margin.def: Free cash flow divided by revenue—tells you what percentage of sales actually turns into distributable cash.
Free Cash Flow Margin.whyThisMatters: High FCF margin means the business is capital-efficient and generates real cash, not just accounting profits.

Gain on Sale of Assets.def: A one-time income boost when you sell property or investments for more than their book value.
Gain on Sale of Assets.whyThisMatters: It's non-recurring, so you should strip it out when analyzing normalized earnings or forecasting.

Gross Profit Bridge.def: Breaks down what caused gross profit to change period-over-period—volume, price/mix, or input costs.
Gross Profit Bridge.whyThisMatters: It tells you whether profit growth came from selling more units, raising prices, or cutting costs.

Impairment Loss.def: A non-cash charge taken when an asset's fair value drops below what it's carried at on the books.
Impairment Loss.whyThisMatters: Common after acquisitions that underperform—it signals overpayment or deteriorating business fundamentals.

Indirect Method Cash Flow.def: Starts with net income, then adjusts for non-cash stuff like depreciation and changes in working capital to show operating cash flow.
Indirect Method Cash Flow.whyThisMatters: It's the most common cash flow format—understanding it helps you reconcile profit to cash.

Intercompany Elimination.def: When consolidating financials, you remove sales and transactions between subsidiaries to avoid double-counting revenue or assets.
Intercompany Elimination.whyThisMatters: Without eliminations, consolidated statements would overstate revenue and assets from internal shuffling.

Accreted Value.def: For bonds sold at a discount (like zero-coupon bonds), it's the original price plus the interest that's accrued over time, bringing it closer to face value.
Accreted Value.whyThisMatters: Understanding accreted value matters when you're modeling OID bonds or PIK notes in LBOs and distressed situations.

Capital Return.def: When an investment pays you back some of your original money — not profits or gains, just the cash you put in initially. Think of it like getting part of your deposit back.
Capital Return.whyThisMatters: You're taxed differently on money you already owned versus actual gains, so knowing the difference keeps more cash in your pocket.

Controlled Auction.def: A sale process where the seller picks who can bid, controls what info they see, and sets the timeline to pit buyers against each other for the best price.
Controlled Auction.whyThisMatters: If you're recruiting for investment banking or private equity, you'll need to know the playbook — teaser, pitch deck, management presentations, final bids.

Debt Service.def: The total cash a company has to pay each period to cover both interest and any principal payments on its loans.
Debt Service.whyThisMatters: Companies with too much debt can't invest in growth or survive rough patches — lenders watch this number obsessively.

Equity Value Bridge.def: The math that takes you from enterprise value to equity value by subtracting debt, preferred shares, and other claims that aren't common stock.
Equity Value Bridge.whyThisMatters: Screwing this up in an interview or model is one of the fastest ways to look unprepared — it shows up in every M&A and LBO case.

Exit Yield.def: The yield you assume a bond or credit investment will have when you sell it, which tells you what price you'll get at exit.
Exit Yield.whyThisMatters: Your entire return in leveraged or distressed debt hinges on this assumption — change it slightly and your IRR swings wildly.

Funded Debt.def: Long-term debt with over a year until it's due — things like term loans and bonds that finance big projects or acquisitions.
Funded Debt.whyThisMatters: Lenders use funded debt-to-EBITDA as the main gauge of leverage, and it's central to every LBO model you'll build.

Gross IRR.def: The return a private equity fund made before taking out fees, expenses, and the cut (carry) that goes to fund managers.
Gross IRR.whyThisMatters: Funds love to brag about gross IRR in marketing decks, but you need to check net IRR to see what investors actually pocketed.

Implied Valuation.def: What a company's worth based on what similar companies or deals traded for — you take a multiple and apply it to their financials.
Implied Valuation.whyThisMatters: Bankers use this to reality-check fancy DCF models — if comps say $500M and your DCF says $1B, something's off.

Net IRR.def: The actual return investors get from a private equity fund after all fees, expenses, and the managers' cut are taken out.
Net IRR.whyThisMatters: This is the only number that matters when comparing PE funds to the stock market — gross IRR is just marketing fluff.
